---
title: "Which Countries Are Most Vulnerable to AI-Driven Economic Disruption?"
subtitle: "A Global Comparative Analysis —  Midterm Project, Advanced Modelling"
author: "Abdullah Tadmuri"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: zenburn
    code_folding: show
    fig_width: 11
    fig_height: 8
    css: style_updated.css
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center", dpi = 130
)

# ---- Global config ----
# START/END define the panel window.
# REF_YEAR is set in {r feature-engineering} after data coverage is known —
# it equals the year with the most complete-case observations.
START <- 2015
END   <- 2025
```

<div class="project-meta">
  <span class="meta-item"><strong>Scope:</strong> Global — all World Bank member countries</span>
  <span class="meta-sep">|</span>
  <span class="meta-item"><strong>Method:</strong> PCA + K-Means · Hierarchical · GMM Clustering</span>
  <span class="meta-sep">|</span>
  <span class="meta-item"><strong>Panel:</strong> 2015–2025</span>
  <span class="meta-sep">|</span>
  <span class="meta-item"><strong>Course:</strong> Advanced Modelling · UC3M · 2025–2026</span>
  <span class="meta-sep">|</span>
  <span class="meta-item"><strong>Source:</strong> World Bank WDI + Oxford AI Readiness Index</span>
</div>

## Abstract

This study identifies which countries are most structurally vulnerable to AI-driven economic disruption using a data-driven, unsupervised learning approach applied to World Bank WDI data (2015–2025). Eleven structural indicators spanning innovation capacity, human capital, digital infrastructure, labour market health, and employment composition are reduced via Principal Component Analysis and then classified using three independent clustering methods — K-Means, Hierarchical (Ward.D2), and Gaussian Mixture Models. Cross-method agreement is strong, and the resulting typology robustly distinguishes structurally resilient economies (high R&D, digital connectivity, and tertiary education) from structurally exposed economies (high agricultural employment, youth unemployment, and weak digital infrastructure). A composite Vulnerability Index — validated externally against the Oxford Insights Government AI Readiness Index 2025 — reveals that vulnerability clusters systematically along the global development hierarchy, though meaningful within-tier heterogeneity exists. Sensitivity analyses confirm ranking stability across alternative index specifications.

# 1. Introduction

Artificial intelligence is no longer a distant prospect — it is actively reshaping industries, displacing routine tasks, and creating entirely new categories of work. But this transformation is not hitting every country equally. A software engineer in Stockholm and a subsistence farmer in sub-Saharan Africa face profoundly different futures, and the structural characteristics of their national economies — investment in research, educational attainment, digital infrastructure, labour market composition — will largely determine how smoothly each country navigates the transition.

This study asks a deceptively simple question with profound policy implications: *Which countries worldwide are most exposed to AI-driven economic disruption, and what makes some nations structurally more resilient than others?*

## 1.1 Research Question

**Which countries are most vulnerable to AI-driven economic disruption, and does vulnerability cluster systematically along the global development divide — separating high-income, middle-income, and low-income economies?**

To answer this, we take a data-driven, unsupervised learning approach. Rather than imposing a pre-defined vulnerability framework, we let the data reveal the underlying structure of the global economy. We use **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** to reduce a set of correlated structural indicators (11 variables) into a smaller set of interpretable dimensions, and then apply **three independent clustering methods** — K-Means, Hierarchical, and Model-Based (Gaussian Mixture) — to group countries with similar structural profiles. The convergence (or divergence) of these methods provides a robust, multi-validated picture of the global economic landscape in the age of AI.

The analysis targets **all World Bank member countries** for which sufficient data is available across the 2015–2025 panel. Countries are stratified by the World Bank's income classification into three development tiers: **Advanced Economies** (high-income), **Emerging Economies** (upper and lower middle income), and **Developing Economies** (low-income). This three-tier structure — following the standard IMF and World Bank classification used in flagship reports such as the *World Economic Outlook* — allows us to ask whether the vulnerability patterns identified by our unsupervised methods mirror, or diverge from, the global income hierarchy, and to generate policy recommendations that are sensitive to the very different capacities of richer and poorer nations to adapt to technological disruption.

## 1.2 Data Sources

Our analysis draws on three complementary data sources, each contributing different dimensions of the vulnerability picture:

| Source | Variables | Type |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------|
| World Bank WDI | R&D intensity, high-tech exports, tertiary education, GDP per capita, GDP growth, labour force participation, internet users, unemployment (total & youth), agricultural employment, industrial employment | Panel (2015–2025) |
| Kaggle | AI automation rate, AI readiness score (synthetic) | Panel (2015–2025) |
| Oxford Insights | Government AI Readiness Index 2025 (6 pillars) | Cross-section (2025) |

The **World Bank World Development Indicators (WDI)** database is our exclusive primary source, providing internationally standardised macroeconomic, human capital, digital infrastructure, labour market, and employment structure variables for virtually every country in the world. Sourcing all structural indicators from a single, unified database eliminates the inconsistencies and coverage gaps that arise from combining multiple national statistical agencies. The **Kaggle** dataset supplements these with AI-specific synthetic metrics (treated as illustrative only). The **Oxford Insights** Government AI Readiness Index adds a policy dimension and, crucially, is itself a global index covering approximately 160 countries — making it more useful here than in a purely European analysis.

Sources: World Bank DataBank (databank.worldbank.org); Oxford Insights (2025) *Government AI Readiness Index*; Kaggle *Global AI Workforce Automation 2015–2025* dataset.

## 1.3 Literature Review

The question of how automation and artificial intelligence reshape labour markets has been the subject of a rapidly growing literature since the seminal contribution of Frey and Osborne (2017), who estimated that 47% of US occupations were at high risk of computerisation. Their task-based framework — distinguishing routine from non-routine, cognitive from manual tasks — has since been applied to dozens of national labour markets and remains the conceptual foundation for automation risk assessment. However, Autor (2015) offered an important counterpoint, arguing that automation historically creates new tasks and occupations even as it destroys old ones, and that the net employment effect depends on institutional and educational factors that vary across countries.

Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) formalised this "automation versus new tasks" framework, showing that countries' capacity to generate new labour-intensive tasks determines whether automation leads to net job gains or losses. Their model predicts that countries with strong educational systems, R&D capacity, and digital infrastructure are better positioned to create complementary new tasks — precisely the structural buffers our analysis operationalises as "resilience." Brynjolfsson and McAfee (2014) emphasised that the current wave of AI-driven automation differs from previous technological revolutions in its speed, breadth, and ability to substitute for cognitive as well as manual tasks, making the transition challenge more acute for countries that lack adaptive capacity.

At the macro level, several studies have used PCA and clustering methods to construct country typologies based on development indicators. The World Bank's annual income classification (World Bank 2024) provides a simple but widely used three- or four-tier stratification, while composite indices like the Oxford Insights Government AI Readiness Index (2025) and the World Economic Forum's Future of Jobs Report (2023) offer more nuanced assessments. Our analysis bridges these approaches: we use the structural indicators that underpin income classification but let unsupervised methods reveal whether the resulting groupings align with — or diverge from — the conventional development hierarchy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Setup & Data Collection

This section handles the technical groundwork: loading R packages, defining helper functions, and pulling raw data from the World Bank API. The entire data collection pipeline is fully reproducible —running this code from a fresh R session will automatically fetch the latest available data and produce a unified panel ready for analysis.

We load packages spanning five categories: **data wrangling** (tidyverse, janitor, countrycode), **API access** (WDI for World Bank), **dimensionality reduction and clustering** (FactoMineR, factoextra, NbClust, cluster, mclust), **visualisation** (corrplot, dendextend, ggrepel), and **geospatial mapping** (sf, rnaturalearth).

```{r packages}
# Packages used in this notebook.
# IMPORTANT: Do NOT install packages inside the knitted report (grader machines may be offline).
pkgs <- c(
  "tidyverse", "janitor", "WDI", "countrycode",
  "FactoMineR", "factoextra", "NbClust", "cluster", "mclust",
  "psych", "dendextend", "corrplot",
  "sf", "rnaturalearth", "rnaturalearthdata", "ggrepel",
  "viridis", "gridExtra", "readxl",
  "knitr", "kableExtra",
  "plotly", "GGally", "ggalluvial", "MVN"
)

missing_pkgs <- setdiff(pkgs, rownames(installed.packages()))
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  stop(
    "Missing required packages: ", paste(missing_pkgs, collapse = ", "),
    "
Install them once (outside this notebook), then re-knit."
  )
}

invisible(lapply(pkgs, library, character.only = TRUE))
set.seed(123)
```


```{r helpers}
# Standardize to z-scores (used throughout)
z <- function(x) as.numeric(scale(x))

# Map World Bank income group to a three-tier development classification
income_to_tier <- function(income_group) {
  case_when(
    income_group == "High income"                                          ~ "Advanced Economies",
    income_group %in% c("Upper middle income", "Lower middle income")     ~ "Emerging Economies",
    income_group == "Low income"                                           ~ "Developing Economies",
    TRUE                                                                   ~ NA_character_
  )
}

# Colorblind-safe tier palette (Okabe–Ito) — defined once, reused everywhere.
# Advanced = blue, Emerging = amber, Developing = green.
tier_pal <- c(
  "Advanced Economies"   = "#0072B2",
  "Emerging Economies"   = "#E69F00",
  "Developing Economies" = "#009E73"
)

```

## 2.1 World Bank WDI —Structural Indicators (All Countries)

The World Bank's World Development Indicators (WDI) database is our sole primary source of structural data. By sourcing all indicators from a single database, we avoid the cross-source inconsistencies that arise when merging national statistical agencies with different methodologies, coverage periods, and country definitions.

We retrieve eleven indicators that collectively capture a country's capacity to generate, absorb, and benefit from technological innovation, alongside its exposure to automation-driven disruption:

- **R&D Intensity** (% of GDP): Measures national commitment to research and development —a direct proxy for innovation capacity and the ability to create AI rather than merely be displaced by it.
- **High-Tech Exports** (% of manufactured exports): Reflects the economy's current positioning in knowledge-intensive trade —a signal of structural sophistication.
- **Tertiary Education** (% of population 25+ with at least a bachelor's degree): Captures the human capital base available to adapt to AI-driven changes.
- **GDP per Capita** (constant 2015 USD): A broad measure of economic development and the fiscal capacity to invest in adjustment policies.
- **GDP Growth** (%): Captures economic dynamism and recent trajectory.
- **Labour Force Participation** (%): Measures how much of the working-age population is economically active.
- **Internet Users** (% of population): A direct measure of digital infrastructure penetration at the population level —countries where the internet is widespread have a larger pool of digitally literate workers.
- **Unemployment Rate** (% of total labour force, ILO modelled estimate): A key labour market health indicator.
- **Youth Unemployment Rate** (% of labour force ages 15–24): High youth unemployment signals labour market rigidity and weak absorptive capacity —both of which amplify disruption risk.
- **Agricultural Employment** (% of total employment): The share of the workforce in agriculture —a sector characterised by low digitalisation, high manual-task intensity, and significant automation exposure.
- **Industrial Employment** (% of total employment): The share in industry (mining, manufacturing, construction, utilities) —a sector with large and growing automation exposure, particularly in assembly-line and repetitive manufacturing tasks.

The `extra = TRUE` flag retrieves additional country metadata including the World Bank income group and geographic region, which we use to define our development tier classification.

```{r fetch-wdi}
# Disk-based caching (items 6 & 7):
#   First run  (internet required): fetches from WDI API → saves data/wdi_START_END.rds
#   Later runs (offline-safe):      loads the RDS directly, skipping the API entirely.
# This guarantees reproducibility on grader machines without internet access.

wdi_cache_file <- file.path("data", paste0("wdi_", START, "_", END, ".rds"))

target_indicators <- c(
  RD_Intensity   = "GB.XPD.RSDV.GD.ZS",
  HiTech_Exports = "TX.VAL.TECH.MF.ZS",
  Tertiary_Edu   = "SE.TER.CUAT.BA.ZS",
  GDP_pc         = "NY.GDP.PCAP.KD",
  GDP_Growth     = "NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG",
  Labor_Part     = "SL.TLF.CACT.ZS",
  Internet_Users = "IT.NET.USER.ZS",
  Unemp_Total    = "SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS",
  Unemp_Youth    = "SL.UEM.1524.ZS",
  Agric_Empl     = "SL.AGR.EMPL.ZS",
  Industry_Empl  = "SL.IND.EMPL.ZS"
)

if (file.exists(wdi_cache_file)) {
  wdi_fetched <- readRDS(wdi_cache_file)
  cat("WDI panel loaded from disk cache:", wdi_cache_file, "\n")
} else {
  cat("Fetching WDI data from API (internet required on first run)...\n")

  wdi_fetched <- tryCatch(
    WDI(
      indicator = target_indicators,
      country   = "all",
      start     = START,
      end       = END,
      extra     = TRUE
    ),
    error = function(e) {
      stop(
        "WDI API call failed. Run at least once with internet access to build the disk cache.\n",
        "Error: ", conditionMessage(e)
      )
    }
  )

  # If any indicator was not returned, add it as NA rather than crashing (item 7)
  for (nm in names(target_indicators)) {
    if (!nm %in% names(wdi_fetched)) {
      warning("Indicator '", nm, "' not returned by WDI API — column added as NA.")
      wdi_fetched[[nm]] <- NA_real_
    }
  }

  saveRDS(wdi_fetched, wdi_cache_file)
  cat("WDI data saved to disk cache:", wdi_cache_file, "\n")
}

wdi_raw <- wdi_fetched |>
  # Remove regional and income-group aggregates; keep country-level observations only
  filter(
    !is.na(income),
    income != "Aggregates",
    str_length(iso3c) == 3
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Development_Tier = income_to_tier(income),
    Development_Tier = factor(
      Development_Tier,
      levels = c("Advanced Economies", "Emerging Economies", "Developing Economies")
    )
  ) |>
  select(iso3c, country, year, region, income, Development_Tier,
         RD_Intensity, HiTech_Exports, Tertiary_Edu,
         GDP_pc, GDP_Growth, Labor_Part,
         Internet_Users, Unemp_Total, Unemp_Youth,
         Agric_Empl, Industry_Empl)

cat("WDI panel:", nrow(wdi_raw), "rows,",
    n_distinct(wdi_raw$iso3c), "countries,",
    min(wdi_raw$year), "-", max(wdi_raw$year), "\n")
cat("Development Tier breakdown (most recent year):\n")
wdi_raw |>
  filter(year == max(year, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  count(Development_Tier) |>
  print()
```

## 2.2 Country Classification & Scope

The World Bank income group —retrieved above via `extra = TRUE` —provides our primary country stratification. We define three development tiers using the IMF and World Bank's standard income-threshold classification, which is recalculated annually using GNI per capita under the Atlas method:

| Development Tier | World Bank Income Group | Approx. GNI per Capita (2023) | Representative Countries |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Advanced Economies | High income | > $13,845 | OECD members, wealthy Gulf states, Singapore, South Korea |
| Emerging Economies | Upper + Lower middle | $1,136–$13,845 | China, Brazil, India, Indonesia, South Africa, Mexico |
| Developing Economies | Low income | < $1,136 | Sub-Saharan Africa (many), parts of South/Southeast Asia |

This terminology follows the IMF *World Economic Outlook* and World Bank *Global Economic Prospects* —the standard references in international economics. It is descriptive rather than evaluative, and it avoids the geopolitical connotations of older "world" numbering systems.

## 2.3 Supplementary: Kaggle & Oxford Insights

We supplement the official World Bank statistics with two additional sources. The **Kaggle AI Workforce Automation** dataset provides country-level estimates of automation rates and AI readiness scores. It is important to note that this dataset is *synthetically generated* —the values are modelled rather than observed —so we treat it strictly as an illustrative supplement. The **Oxford Insights Government AI Readiness Index** adds a policy dimension, scoring approximately 160 governments on their preparedness for AI across six pillars (governance, infrastructure, data availability, human capital, innovation, and government capacity). Unlike the European analysis it was previously used in, here its global coverage is a genuine asset.

**Note on data files:** The supplementary CSV files should be placed in a `data/` subfolder within the same directory as this Rmd file. The zip submission should include both CSVs.

```{r supplementary}
# Kaggle AI automation panel (synthetic —for external validation only)
ai_auto <- tryCatch({
  read_csv(file.path("data", "global_ai_workforce_automation_2015_2025.csv"),
           show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    clean_names() |>
    transmute(
      iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c", warn = FALSE),
      year  = as.integer(year),
      automation_rate    = automation_rate_percent,
      ai_readiness_score = ai_readiness_score
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(iso3c))
}, error = function(e) {
  message("Kaggle file not found in data/ —external validation will be skipped. ", e$message)
  tibble(iso3c = character(), year = integer(),
         automation_rate = numeric(), ai_readiness_score = numeric())
})

# Oxford Insights Government AI Readiness Index 2025 (global cross-section)
ai_readiness <- tryCatch({
  raw_ox <- read_csv(file.path("data", "ai_readiness_index.csv"),
                     show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    clean_names()   # normalises column names to snake_case (e.g., "Overall Score" -> "overall_score")

  # Robustly resolve ISO3 codes whether the file has them or only country names
  if ("iso3c" %in% names(raw_ox)) {
    raw_ox <- raw_ox |> mutate(iso3c = as.character(iso3c))
  } else {
    raw_ox <- raw_ox |>
      mutate(iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c", warn = FALSE))
  }

  # Keep iso3c + ALL available Oxford pillar columns (item 26)
  oxford_pillar_cols <- intersect(
    c("overall_score", "policy_capacity", "ai_infrastructure",
      "governance", "public_sector_adoption", "development_diffusion", "resilience"),
    names(raw_ox)
  )

  raw_ox |>
    select(iso3c, all_of(oxford_pillar_cols)) |>
    filter(!is.na(iso3c))
}, error = function(e) {
  message("Oxford AI Readiness file not found in data/ — external validation will be skipped. ", e$message)
  tibble(iso3c = character(), overall_score = numeric())
})

cat("Kaggle records loaded:", nrow(ai_auto), "\n")
cat("Oxford AI Readiness records loaded:", nrow(ai_readiness), "\n")
```

## 2.4 Merge Master Panel

With the primary and supplementary data loaded, we merge them into a single master panel dataset — one row per country per year. Joins are performed on ISO-3 country codes and year. Supplementary datasets are joined with `left_join()` so that their absence does not reduce the core panel. After each join we assert that no duplicate (iso3c, year) rows have been created — duplicates would indicate a join key mismatch and must be fixed explicitly, not silently averaged away.

```{r merge}
# item 9: left joins preserve the core WDI panel; supplementary data added by key.
# We assert uniqueness after each join — duplicate (iso3c, year) rows indicate
# a broken join key and must be fixed explicitly, not silently averaged away.
all_data <- wdi_raw |>
  left_join(ai_auto,      by = c("iso3c", "year")) |>
  left_join(ai_readiness, by = "iso3c")

dup_check <- all_data |> count(iso3c, year) |> filter(n > 1)
if (nrow(dup_check) > 0) {
  stop(
    nrow(dup_check), " duplicate (iso3c, year) pairs detected after merges.\n",
    "First offenders: ",
    paste(with(head(dup_check, 5), paste0(iso3c, "/", year)), collapse = ", "),
    "\nFix the join keys — do not silently average duplicates."
  )
}

cat("Master panel:", nrow(all_data), "rows,",
    n_distinct(all_data$iso3c), "countries,",
    min(all_data$year), "-", max(all_data$year), "\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. Pre-Processing & Descriptive Analysis

Before applying any statistical technique, we must thoroughly understand the structure, quality, and distributional properties of our data. This section serves as a diagnostic checkpoint: we examine the data dimensions, assess missing values, define our analysis variables, inspect distributions, detect outliers, and explore the correlation structure. Each step informs critical decisions downstream.

## 3.1 Data Structure

A quick structural overview confirms the panel format and verifies that all expected variables are present.

```{r structure}
glimpse(all_data)
```

## 3.2 Missing Data Analysis

Missing data is an unavoidable reality when working with global data. High-income countries tend to report comprehensively to the World Bank, while low-income countries —where reporting infrastructure is weaker —often have gaps in R&D spending, tertiary education attainment, and high-technology trade statistics. Understanding the pattern of missingness is critical: if gaps are systematic by development tier (as is likely), complete-case analysis may underrepresent low-income countries.

```{r missing-data, fig.height=6}
analysis_core <- c("RD_Intensity", "HiTech_Exports", "Tertiary_Edu",
                   "GDP_pc", "GDP_Growth", "Labor_Part",
                   "Internet_Users", "Unemp_Total", "Unemp_Youth",
                   "Agric_Empl", "Industry_Empl")

miss_pct <- all_data |>
  select(all_of(analysis_core)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), \(x) mean(is.na(x)) * 100)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Pct_Missing") |>
  arrange(desc(Pct_Missing))

ggplot(miss_pct, aes(reorder(Variable, Pct_Missing), Pct_Missing)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", Pct_Missing)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Missing Data by Variable (Full Panel, All Countries)",
       x = NULL, y = "% Missing") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0, max(miss_pct$Pct_Missing) * 1.2)
```

```{r missing-by-tier, fig.height=5}
# Missingness by development tier highlights the systematic data gap
all_data |>
  filter(!is.na(Development_Tier)) |>
  select(Development_Tier, all_of(analysis_core)) |>
  group_by(Development_Tier) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), \(x) mean(is.na(x)) * 100), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_longer(-Development_Tier, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Pct_Missing") |>
  ggplot(aes(Variable, Pct_Missing, fill = Development_Tier)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  labs(title = "Missing Data by Variable and Development Tier",
       subtitle = "Low-income countries have systematically higher missingness for R&D and education variables",
       x = NULL, y = "% Missing", fill = "Tier") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r missing-table}
miss_pct |>
  kable(digits = 1, col.names = c("Variable", "% Missing"),
        caption = "Overall Missing Data by Variable") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## 3.3 Feature Engineering & Complete-Case Filter

The eleven WDI variables used here are expressed as ratios or percentages of well-defined totals (ILO modelled estimates, total employment, total population), making them directly comparable across countries of all sizes. Germany and Luxembourg are compared on equal footing because all variables capture *structure*, not scale. This is a key advantage of the global WDI approach.

We define our final analysis variable set and apply a **complete-case filter**: only country-year observations with valid values on all eleven analysis variables are retained. While this reduces sample size —particularly for low-income countries where R&D and tertiary education reporting is sparse —it ensures that PCA operates on a consistent, fully-observed matrix, which is a mathematical requirement for accurate eigenvalue decomposition.

```{r feature-engineering}
analysis_vars <- c(
  "RD_Intensity",   # Innovation capacity
  "HiTech_Exports", # Knowledge-economy positioning
  "Tertiary_Edu",   # Human capital
  "GDP_pc",         # Economic development level
  "GDP_Growth",     # Economic dynamism (cyclical)
  "Labor_Part",     # Labour market activity
  "Internet_Users", # Digital infrastructure penetration
  "Unemp_Total",    # Labour market distress (structural)
  "Unemp_Youth",    # Youth labour market vulnerability
  "Agric_Empl",     # Agricultural employment share (ILO % of total)
  "Industry_Empl"   # Industrial employment share (ILO % of total)
)

df_cc <- all_data |>
  filter(year >= START, year <= END,
         !is.na(Development_Tier)) |>
  filter(!if_any(all_of(analysis_vars), is.na))


# Sanity checks: uniqueness of (country, year) after merges
dup_keys <- df_cc |> count(iso3c, year) |> filter(n > 1)
if (nrow(dup_keys) > 0) {
  stop("Duplicate (iso3c, year) rows detected after merges. Investigate joins before proceeding.")
}


cat("Complete-case panel:", nrow(df_cc), "rows,",
    n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c), "countries,",
    length(analysis_vars), "analysis variables\n")
cat("Variables:", paste(analysis_vars, collapse = ", "), "\n")

# Compute reference year here (year with most complete-case observations)
# so it is available to all subsequent chunks including tier-coverage
latest_year <- df_cc |>
  count(year) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  pull(year)
cat("Reference year for cross-sectional analysis:", latest_year, "\n")
```

```{r set-ref-year-after-merge, include=FALSE}

# Use the year with the most complete-case observations (computed above)
REF_YEAR <- latest_year
```


```{r attrition-table}
# Attrition / selection transparency (how the sample changes across filters)
attr_tbl <- tibble(
  step = c(
    "Merged raw panel (all_data)",
    paste0("Year window [", START, "–", END, "]"),
    "With development tier (WB income group available)",
    "Complete-case on analysis variables"
  ),
  rows = c(
    nrow(all_data),
    nrow(all_data |> filter(year >= START, year <= END)),
    nrow(all_data |> filter(year >= START, year <= END, !is.na(Development_Tier))),
    nrow(df_cc)
  ),
  countries = c(
    n_distinct(all_data$iso3c),
    n_distinct(all_data |> filter(year >= START, year <= END) |> pull(iso3c)),
    n_distinct(all_data |> filter(year >= START, year <= END, !is.na(Development_Tier)) |> pull(iso3c)),
    n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)
  )
)

attr_tbl |>
  kable(caption = "Sample attrition across preprocessing steps") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```



**Result:** The final analysis dataset contains `r nrow(df_cc)` observations (country-years), `r n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)` countries, and `r length(analysis_vars)` structural indicators —satisfying the project requirements of $n > 100$ observations and $p \geq 10$ variables.

<div class="stat-grid">
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-value">`r n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Countries</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-value">`r length(analysis_vars)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Variables</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-value">`r as.integer(END - START + 1)`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Years (`r START`–`r END`)</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-value">3</div>
<div class="stat-label">Clustering Methods</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
<div class="stat-value">`r formatC(nrow(df_cc), format = "d", big.mark = ",")`</div>
<div class="stat-label">Observations</div>
</div>
</div>

**A note on the Kaggle and Oxford Insights data:** These datasets were deliberately excluded from the core PCA/clustering pipeline. The Kaggle data is *synthetically generated*, so incorporating it alongside official statistics would contaminate the factor structure with artificial patterns. The Oxford Insights index is *cross-sectional* (one score per country, 2025 only), which is incompatible with our panel-based PCA. Both are used later as **external validators** in Section 5.8.

### Countries Included and Excluded

```{r country-coverage}
# all_data already carries Development_Tier from the WDI fetch —use it directly
all_targeted <- all_data |>
  distinct(iso3c, country, Development_Tier) |>
  arrange(country)

included <- df_cc |>
  distinct(iso3c, country, Development_Tier) |>
  arrange(country)

excluded <- all_data |>
  distinct(iso3c, country, Development_Tier) |>
  filter(!iso3c %in% included$iso3c) |>
  arrange(Development_Tier, country)

cat("Countries in source data:", n_distinct(all_data$iso3c), "\n")
cat("Countries in final analysis:", nrow(included), "\n")
cat("Countries excluded:", nrow(excluded), "\n\n")

excluded |>
  count(Development_Tier, name = "n_excluded") |>
  kable(caption = "Excluded Countries by Development Tier") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

The table above confirms the anticipated pattern: the majority of excluded countries are in the Low-Income tier, primarily because R&D spending and tertiary education attainment data are not systematically reported to the World Bank by the world's least-developed economies. This systematic attrition is acknowledged as a limitation in Section 8.2 —the vulnerability rankings will under-represent the very poorest countries, which may in fact be the most vulnerable.

### Development Tier Coverage in Final Analysis

```{r tier-coverage}
df_cc |>
  filter(year == REF_YEAR)|>
  count(Development_Tier, name = "n_countries") |>
  kable(caption = paste0("Countries in Final Analysis by Development Tier (", latest_year, ")")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## 3.4 Summary Statistics

The descriptive statistics below provide a concise portrait of our analysis variables. For each indicator, we report the mean, standard deviation, quartiles, and range.

```{r summary-stats}
df_cc |>
  select(all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable") |>
  group_by(Variable) |>
  summarise(
    Mean   = mean(value),
    SD     = sd(value),
    Min    = min(value),
    Q1     = quantile(value, 0.25),
    Median = median(value),
    Q3     = quantile(value, 0.75),
    Max    = max(value),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(digits = 2, caption = "Summary Statistics of Analysis Variables") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Mean Values by Development Tier

This pre-PCA comparison already reveals the broad structural differences between development levels that PCA will subsequently formalise.

```{r tier-means, fig.height=9}
df_cc |>
  select(Development_Tier, all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(-Development_Tier, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") |>
  group_by(Development_Tier, Variable) |>
  summarise(Mean = mean(Value), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(Development_Tier, Mean, fill = Development_Tier)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  facet_wrap(~ Variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Mean Variable Values by Development Tier",
       subtitle = "Pre-PCA comparison: structural divergence across the global development hierarchy",
       x = NULL, y = "Mean Value", fill = "Tier") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))
```

## 3.5 Distributions

Examining the distributional shape of each variable is essential for PCA, which works best when variables are roughly normally distributed. Variables like GDP per capita typically show pronounced right skew in a global sample —a handful of very wealthy countries pull the distribution tail rightward. Standardising to z-scores (which PCA does automatically via `scale.unit = TRUE`) corrects for differences in scale but does not eliminate skewness.

```{r distributions, fig.height=11}
df_cc |>
  select(all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") |>
  ggplot(aes(Value)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30,
                 fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(colour = "darkred", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Analysis Variables (All Country-Years)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 3.6 Outlier Detection

We use a z-score criterion (|z| > 3) to flag potential outliers. In a normal distribution, fewer than 0.3% of observations exceed this threshold, so any flagged values genuinely stand out. We retain all observations —extreme but real country-level values (e.g., Singapore's extraordinary high-tech export share, or a Gulf state's very low agricultural employment) represent genuine structural characteristics, not data errors.

```{r outliers}
z_outliers <- df_cc |>
  select(all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), z)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), \(x) sum(abs(x) > 3))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Outliers_z3") |>
  arrange(desc(Outliers_z3))

z_outliers |>
  kable(caption = "Number of Extreme Observations (|z| > 3) per Variable") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## 3.7 Correlation Analysis

The correlation matrix is the single most important diagnostic for PCA suitability. PCA works by compressing correlated variables into fewer dimensions —if variables are uncorrelated, there is nothing to compress. Conversely, strong correlations among groups of variables indicate redundant information that PCA can efficiently consolidate.

The heatmap below displays Pearson correlation coefficients between all analysis variables.

```{r correlation, fig.height=8}
cor_mat <- cor(df_cc[, analysis_vars], use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(cor_mat, method = "color", type = "upper",
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.65,
         tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45, tl.cex = 0.8,
         title = "Correlation Matrix of Analysis Variables", mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0))
```

**Key patterns from the global correlation structure:**

- **A "development" cluster**: GDP per capita correlates strongly with R&D intensity (r=`r round(cor_mat["GDP_pc","RD_Intensity"], 2)`), internet users (r=`r round(cor_mat["GDP_pc","Internet_Users"], 2)`), and tertiary education (r=`r round(cor_mat["GDP_pc","Tertiary_Edu"], 2)`). This mirrors the well-established co-movement of economic development indicators globally —wealthier countries simultaneously invest more in R&D, educate more of their population to degree level, and have more of their citizens online.
- **A "structural vulnerability" cluster**: Agricultural employment and total unemployment are positively correlated (r=`r round(cor_mat["Agric_Empl","Unemp_Total"], 2)`), and both are strongly negatively associated with GDP per capita. Countries with large agricultural sectors tend to be poorer, have weaker labour markets, and show lower digital infrastructure.
- **A "youth labour market" signal**: Youth unemployment correlates strongly with total unemployment (r=`r round(cor_mat["Unemp_Youth","Unemp_Total"], 2)`) and negatively with GDP per capita, suggesting that young people in low-income countries face especially difficult labour market entry.

These strong, interpretable correlation structures confirm that the data is well-suited for PCA — there is substantial shared variance to be compressed into a smaller number of meaningful dimensions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 4. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

Principal Component Analysis is the backbone of our dimensionality reduction strategy. With `r length(analysis_vars)` correlated structural indicators, direct analysis would be unwieldy and prone to multicollinearity issues. PCA transforms the original variables into a new set of **principal components** — uncorrelated linear combinations ordered by the amount of variance they capture. The first component explains the most variance; each subsequent component captures the most remaining variance orthogonal to all preceding components.

The practical benefit is twofold. First, PCA allows us to *visualise* the multi-dimensional structure of global economies on simple 2D plots. Second, clustering in PCA space reduces noise from minor components and avoids the "curse of dimensionality" — a phenomenon where distance-based methods degrade as the number of dimensions grows, because all pairs of points become nearly equidistant.

**Important methodological note:** PCA is performed on the **reference-year cross-section** (`r REF_YEAR`), not the full panel, to avoid pseudo-replication (the same country appearing up to 11 times would inflate eigenvalues and violate the independence assumption). This ensures that each observation in the PCA is an independent country, and that the resulting eigenstructure, loadings, and scores are not contaminated by within-country temporal variation. The reference year is selected as the year with the most complete-case observations.

PCA is performed on **standardised (z-scored) data** (`scale.unit = TRUE`). Standardisation is essential here because the 11 variables are measured on radically different scales — GDP per capita ranges in thousands of constant US dollars, while employment shares and internet penetration are bounded percentages. Without standardisation, high-variance variables (e.g., GDP per capita) would dominate the first principal component regardless of their conceptual importance. By converting each variable to z-scores (mean 0, SD 1) before decomposition, every indicator contributes equally to the analysis.

```{r pca-cross-section, include=FALSE}
# Create the reference-year cross-section for PCA (one row per country).
# This eliminates pseudo-replication from the panel.
df_ref <- df_cc |> filter(year == REF_YEAR)
cor_mat_ref <- cor(df_ref[, analysis_vars], use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
cat("Reference-year cross-section for PCA:", nrow(df_ref), "countries\n")
```

## 4.1 Data Suitability

We apply three formal diagnostic tests before computing PCA:

- The **Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO)** measure of sampling adequacy quantifies the proportion of variance among variables that might be caused by underlying common factors. Values above 0.5 are acceptable, above 0.7 good, and above 0.8 excellent (Kaiser 1974).
- **Bartlett's test of sphericity** tests the null hypothesis that the correlation matrix is an identity matrix (all variables independent). A significant result ($p < 0.001$) confirms that meaningful correlations exist to exploit.
- **Mardia's test of multivariate normality** assesses whether the data distribution satisfies the multivariate normality assumption underlying the GMM clustering applied later (Section 5.4).

```{r kmo-bartlett}
kmo_result     <- KMO(cor_mat_ref)
bartlett_result <- cortest.bartlett(cor_mat_ref, n = nrow(df_ref))

cat("KMO overall MSA:", round(kmo_result$MSA, 3), "\n")
cat("Bartlett's test: chi-sq =", round(bartlett_result$chisq, 1),
    ", df =", bartlett_result$df,
    ", p =", format.pval(bartlett_result$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")
```

```{r mardia-test}
# Mardia's test for multivariate normality (relevant for GMM assumptions)
mardia_res <- MVN::mvn(df_ref[, analysis_vars], mvn_test = "mardia")
mardia_res$multivariate_normality |>
  kable(caption = "Mardia's Test of Multivariate Normality (reference-year cross-section)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

Both KMO and Bartlett's tests should confirm strong suitability. The globally correlated nature of development indicators (education, income, R&D, internet, employment structure) typically produces KMO values well above 0.8 in samples of this kind. Mardia's test is reported for transparency — multivariate normality is rarely satisfied in real-world economic data, and GMMs are known to be reasonably robust to moderate departures (Fraley & Raftery 2002).

## 4.2 PCA Computation

```{r pca-compute}
# PCA on the reference-year cross-section (one row per country).
# ncp set to the full number of variables to ensure no components are truncated.
pca_res <- PCA(df_ref[, analysis_vars], scale.unit = TRUE,
               ncp = length(analysis_vars), graph = FALSE)

eig_tbl <- get_eigenvalue(pca_res) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("PC") |>
  as_tibble()

eig_tbl |>
  kable(digits = 2,
        col.names = c("Component", "Eigenvalue", "% Variance", "Cumulative %"),
        caption = "PCA Eigenvalues — Full Decomposition (reference-year cross-section)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## 4.3 Scree Plot & Parallel Analysis

The scree plot visualises eigenvalues in descending order. We apply two complementary retention rules:

1. The **Kaiser criterion** (retain all components with eigenvalue $\geq 1$, shown as the red dashed line): a component must capture more variance than any single original standardised variable to be worth retaining.
2. **Horn's Parallel Analysis** (1965): compares observed eigenvalues against eigenvalues from 5,000 random datasets of the same dimensions. Components are retained only if their observed eigenvalue exceeds the 95th percentile of the random distribution — a more stringent and widely recommended criterion than Kaiser alone (Hayton, Allen & Scarpello 2004).

```{r scree, fig.height=5}
ggplot(eig_tbl, aes(x = seq_len(nrow(eig_tbl)), y = eigenvalue)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(variance.percent, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(nrow(eig_tbl)),
                     labels = paste0("PC", seq_len(nrow(eig_tbl)))) +
  labs(title = "Scree Plot with Kaiser Criterion (eigenvalue = 1)",
       x = "Principal Component", y = "Eigenvalue") +
  theme_minimal()

n_retain <- sum(eig_tbl$eigenvalue >= 1)
cum_var  <- eig_tbl$cumulative.variance.percent[n_retain]
cat("Components retained (Kaiser criterion):", n_retain,
    "| Cumulative variance:", round(cum_var, 1), "%\n")
```

```{r parallel-analysis, fig.height=5}
# Horn's Parallel Analysis — gold standard for PCA retention
set.seed(123)
pa_res <- psych::fa.parallel(df_ref[, analysis_vars], fa = "pc",
                              n.iter = 5000, show.legend = TRUE,
                              main = "Parallel Analysis — Observed vs Random Eigenvalues")

n_retain_pa <- pa_res$ncomp
cat("Parallel Analysis suggests retaining:", n_retain_pa, "components\n")
cat("Kaiser criterion suggests retaining:", n_retain, "components\n")

# Use the more conservative of the two if they differ
if (n_retain_pa != n_retain) {
  cat("Using Kaiser criterion (", n_retain, ") for downstream analysis;",
      "Parallel Analysis (", n_retain_pa, ") noted for comparison.\n")
}
```

By the **Kaiser criterion**, we retain **`r n_retain` components** explaining **`r round(cum_var, 1)`%** of total variance. Horn's Parallel Analysis `r if(n_retain_pa == n_retain) "independently confirms this choice" else paste0("suggests ", n_retain_pa, " components, which is noted as a sensitivity check")`.

## 4.4 Loadings & Interpretation

**Loadings** are the correlations between original variables and each principal component. High positive loadings indicate the variable increases with the component; high negative loadings indicate an inverse relationship. We report `$coord` from FactoMineR, which equals the variable-PC correlations in a standardised PCA (`scale.unit = TRUE`), providing directly interpretable factor loadings.

```{r loadings}
load_df <- get_pca_var(pca_res)$coord |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  rownames_to_column("Variable") |>
  as_tibble()

# Display loadings for retained components only
load_df |>
  select(Variable, all_of(paste0("Dim.", 1:min(n_retain, 5)))) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(Dim.1))) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = "PCA Loadings: Variable–Component Correlations (retained components)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

**Interpretation of retained components (threshold: |loading| > 0.4):**

Each component is given a substantive name based on its dominant loadings. The name reflects what "high" vs. "low" scores mean in terms of real economic structure.

```{r interpret-pca, results='asis'}
# Auto-assign a substantive name based on which variables dominate the component
assign_pc_name <- function(pos_vars, neg_vars) {
  all_vars <- c(pos_vars, neg_vars)
  dev_vars   <- c("GDP_pc", "Internet_Users", "Tertiary_Edu", "RD_Intensity", "HiTech_Exports")
  labor_vars <- c("Labor_Part", "Unemp_Total", "Unemp_Youth")
  sector_vars <- c("Agric_Empl", "Industry_Empl")
  dyn_vars   <- c("GDP_Growth")

  if (length(intersect(dev_vars, all_vars)) >= 2)   return("Global Development & Digital Readiness")
  if (length(intersect(labor_vars, all_vars)) >= 2) return("Labour Market Strain")
  if (length(intersect(sector_vars, all_vars)) >= 1 && length(intersect(dyn_vars, all_vars)) >= 1)
    return("Sectoral Composition & Economic Dynamism")
  if ("GDP_Growth" %in% all_vars)                   return("Economic Dynamism")
  if (length(intersect(sector_vars, all_vars)) >= 1) return("Sectoral Composition")
  paste0("Component ", length(pos_vars) + length(neg_vars))
}

for (d in 1:min(n_retain, 3)) {
  col     <- paste0("Dim.", d)
  top_pos <- load_df |> filter(.data[[col]] >  0.4) |> arrange(desc(.data[[col]]))
  top_neg <- load_df |> filter(.data[[col]] < -0.4) |> arrange(.data[[col]])

  pc_label <- assign_pc_name(top_pos$Variable, top_neg$Variable)

  cat(sprintf('\n**PC%d — "%s"** (%.1f%% variance explained)\n\n', d, pc_label,
              eig_tbl$variance.percent[d]))
  if (nrow(top_pos) > 0)
    cat("- **High scores** (above-average):", paste(top_pos$Variable, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  if (nrow(top_neg) > 0)
    cat("- **Low scores** (below-average):", paste(top_neg$Variable, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("\n")
}
```

In global cross-country data, the dominant first component almost invariably captures the **global development axis** —a single underlying dimension that reflects the deep co-movement of income, education, R&D, digital infrastructure, and sectoral composition. Countries scoring high on PC1 tend to be wealthy, educated, R&D-intensive, digitally connected, and have small agricultural sectors; those scoring low show the opposite profile.

## 4.5 Variable Contributions

**Contributions** measure the magnitude of each variable's role in defining each component, expressed as a percentage. The red dashed reference line marks the expected contribution if all variables contributed equally (100% ÷ number of variables).

```{r var-contrib, fig.height=6}
fviz_contrib(pca_res, choice = "var", axes = 1, top = length(analysis_vars),
             title = "Variable Contributions to PC1") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r var-contrib-2, fig.height=6}
fviz_contrib(pca_res, choice = "var", axes = 2, top = length(analysis_vars),
             title = "Variable Contributions to PC2") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 4.6 PCA Variable Map (cos²)

The variable map projects original variables onto the first two principal components. Variables appearing close together are positively correlated; those on opposite sides of the origin are negatively correlated; those at right angles are uncorrelated. The colour gradient represents $\cos^2$ —the quality of each variable's representation in this 2D plane.

```{r pca-var-map, fig.height=7}
fviz_pca_var(pca_res, col.var = "cos2", repel = TRUE,
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             title = "PCA Variable Map —coloured by cos² (quality of 2D representation)")
```

## 4.7 Biplot —Country Positions Coloured by Development Tier

This biplot projects each country's structural profile onto PC1–PC2. Points are coloured by World Bank income tier, allowing us to see immediately whether the data-driven PCA dimensions align with the conventional development hierarchy. If PCA has successfully captured the global development axis in PC1, we would expect a clear left-to-right gradient from Low-Income to High-Income countries.

```{r biplot, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
# PCA scores are already for the reference-year cross-section (df_ref)
ind_scores <- as_tibble(pca_res$ind$coord[, 1:min(n_retain, 5)])
names(ind_scores) <- paste0("PC", seq_along(ind_scores))
ind_scores$row_id <- seq_len(nrow(ind_scores))

df_pca <- df_ref |>
  mutate(row_id = row_number()) |>
  left_join(ind_scores, by = "row_id") |>
  select(-row_id)

ggplot(df_pca, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Development_Tier, label = iso3c)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.8, max.overlaps = 30) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("PCA Biplot — Country Positions (", REF_YEAR, ")"),
    subtitle = "Coloured by World Bank Income Group",
    x = sprintf("PC1 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[1]),
    y = sprintf("PC2 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[2]),
    colour = "Development Tier"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 4.8 Representation Quality (cos²)

The $\cos^2$ statistic measures how accurately each country's position in the 2D biplot represents its full multi-dimensional profile. Values below 0.5 (shown in red) indicate that important aspects of that country's economy are captured by higher-order components, and the 2D position may be misleading.

```{r cos2, fig.height=9}
ind_cos2 <- get_pca_ind(pca_res)$cos2 |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(cos2_12 = Dim.1 + Dim.2, row_id = row_number())

df_cos2 <- df_ref |>
  mutate(row_id = row_number()) |>
  left_join(ind_cos2 |> select(row_id, cos2_12), by = "row_id") |>
  arrange(cos2_12) |>
  mutate(bar_fill = if_else(cos2_12 > 0.5, "steelblue", "tomato"))

ggplot(df_cos2, aes(reorder(iso3c, cos2_12), cos2_12, fill = bar_fill)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "cos² Quality of Representation on PC1–PC2",
       subtitle = "Red bars: poorly represented in 2D (cos² < 0.5) — interpret biplot position with caution",
       x = NULL, y = "cos² (PC1 + PC2)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 5. Clustering

With PCA complete, we now identify **groups** of countries that share similar structural profiles. While PCA revealed the main dimensions of global economic variation, clustering takes the next step: assigning countries to discrete, interpretable groups. We apply three fundamentally different clustering algorithms — **K-Means**, **Hierarchical (agglomerative)**, and **Model-Based (Gaussian Mixture)** — to exploit their complementary assumptions. Agreement across methods provides strong evidence that the groups are genuine features of the data.

All clustering is performed in **PCA space** using all `r n_retain` retained components (those with eigenvalue ≥ 1, explaining `r round(cum_var, 1)`% of variance). Using the full retained set rather than an arbitrary cap ensures no meaningful structural dimension is discarded. Clustering in PCA space eliminates multicollinearity between correlated original features, reduces noise from uninformative minor components, and mitigates the curse of dimensionality — a phenomenon where Euclidean distances become uninformative as the number of raw dimensions grows.

**Distance metric:** All three clustering methods use **Euclidean distance** in the standardised PCA score space. Scale sensitivity — the main pitfall of Euclidean distance on raw variables — is fully handled by the prior PCA standardisation step: every retained component has unit variance, so no single dimension can dominate the distance calculation.

```{r cluster-data}
# df_pca already contains only the reference-year cross-section (one row per country)
# since PCA was fitted on df_ref.
n_pcs <- n_retain  # use all Kaiser-retained components (no arbitrary cap)

df_cluster <- df_pca   # already reference year only

pca_space <- df_cluster |> select(all_of(paste0("PC", 1:n_pcs)))
stopifnot(nrow(pca_space) == n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c))  # one row per country
```


## 5.1 Optimal Number of Clusters ($k$)

We determine $k$ using four independent methods and look for convergence.

### Elbow Method (WSS)

The elbow method plots total within-cluster sum of squares against $k$. The "elbow" — where improvement slows markedly — identifies the value of $k$ beyond which additional clusters explain diminishing additional variance.

```{r elbow, fig.height=5}
k_grid <- 2:8

wss_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(k_grid, \(k) {
  set.seed(123)
  km <- kmeans(pca_space, centers = k, nstart = 50)
  tibble(k = k, WSS = km$tot.withinss)
})

ggplot(wss_tbl, aes(k, WSS)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Elbow Plot (Total Within-Cluster SS)", x = "k", y = "WSS") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Average Silhouette Width

For each observation, the **silhouette coefficient** measures how similar it is to its own cluster versus the nearest alternative cluster. Values range from −1 (misclassified) to +1 (well-matched). The average across all observations is maximised at the optimal $k$.

```{r silhouette-k, fig.height=5}
sil_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(k_grid, \(k) {
  set.seed(123)
  km <- kmeans(pca_space, centers = k, nstart = 50)
  ss <- silhouette(km$cluster, dist(pca_space))
  tibble(k = k, Avg_Silhouette = mean(ss[, "sil_width"]))
})

ggplot(sil_tbl, aes(k, Avg_Silhouette)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Average Silhouette Width by k", x = "k", y = "Avg Silhouette") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Gap Statistic

The **Gap statistic** (Tibshirani, Walther & Hastie, 2001) compares the observed within-cluster variation against the expected variation under a null reference distribution (uniform data with no cluster structure). The optimal $k$ is the smallest value where the observed clustering is substantially tighter than random data. We use 100 bootstrap reference samples.

```{r gap-stat, fig.height=5}
gap_cache <- file.path("data", "gap_stat.rds")
if (file.exists(gap_cache)) {
  gap_res <- readRDS(gap_cache)
  cat("Gap statistic loaded from disk cache.\n")
} else {
  set.seed(123)
  gap_res <- clusGap(as.data.frame(pca_space), FUNcluster = kmeans,
                     nstart = 50, K.max = 8, B = 100)
  saveRDS(gap_res, gap_cache)
}
fviz_gap_stat(gap_res) +
  labs(title = "Gap Statistic (100 bootstrap reference samples)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### NbClust Majority Vote

**NbClust** evaluates 30 different statistical indices for clustering quality and conducts a majority vote on the optimal $k$. This ensemble approach is more robust than any single criterion.

```{r nbclust, fig.height=5}
nbclust_cache <- file.path("data", "nbclust.rds")
if (file.exists(nbclust_cache)) {
  nb <- readRDS(nbclust_cache)
  cat("NbClust result loaded from disk cache.\n")
} else {
  set.seed(123)
  # Suppress NbClust's internal cat() output and its auto-generated
  # Hubert / D-index plots (which would appear as extra unsolicited figures)
  pdf(nullfile())
  invisible(capture.output(
    nb <- NbClust(as.data.frame(pca_space), distance = "euclidean",
                  min.nc = 2, max.nc = 8, method = "kmeans", index = "all")
  ))
  dev.off()
  saveRDS(nb, nbclust_cache)
}

k_votes <- as.numeric(nb$Best.nc[1, ])
k_votes <- k_votes[!is.na(k_votes) & k_votes >= 2 & k_votes <= 8]

vote_tbl <- tibble(k = k_votes) |>
  count(k, name = "Votes") |>
  arrange(desc(Votes))

ggplot(vote_tbl, aes(factor(k), Votes)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Votes), vjust = -0.4) +
  labs(title = "NbClust Majority Vote —Optimal k",
       x = "k", y = "Number of indices voting") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Decision

```{r k-decision}
k_nb  <- vote_tbl |> slice_max(Votes, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> pull(k)
k_sil <- sil_tbl  |> slice_max(Avg_Silhouette, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |> pull(k)

cat("NbClust majority vote: k =", k_nb, "\n")
cat("Best silhouette: k =", k_sil, "\n")

k_final <- k_nb
cat("Selected k =", k_final, "\n")
```

We select **k = `r k_final`** based on the NbClust majority rule, consistent with the elbow and silhouette evidence. In a global sample with high-income, middle-income, and low-income countries, we expect the algorithm to identify clusters that broadly reflect these development tiers —though the unsupervised method may also reveal finer structural distinctions within tiers.

## 5.2 K-Means Clustering

**K-Means** partitions observations into $k$ spherical clusters by iteratively assigning observations to the nearest centroid and recomputing centroids as cluster means. We run with 100 random starting configurations (`nstart = 100`) to avoid local minima.

```{r kmeans}


set.seed(123)

# safety: these must align row-by-row
stopifnot(nrow(df_cluster) == nrow(pca_space))

km_fit <- kmeans(pca_space, centers = k_final, nstart = 100)

df_cluster <- df_cluster |>
  dplyr::mutate(km_cluster = factor(km_fit$cluster))
```

```{r kmeans-plot, fig.height=7}
fviz_cluster(km_fit, data = as.data.frame(pca_space),
             ggtheme = theme_minimal(),
             main = paste("K-Means Clusters (k =", k_final, ") in PCA Space"))
```

```{r silhouette-standard, fig.height=6}
# Standard silhouette plot — canonical clustering diagnostic (Kaufman & Rousseeuw 1990)
fviz_silhouette(silhouette(km_fit$cluster, dist(pca_space)),
                main = paste("Silhouette Plot — K-Means (k =", k_final, ")")) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r sanity-clustered}
stopifnot(exists("df_cluster"))
stopifnot("km_cluster" %in% names(df_cluster))
cat("Clustered sample:", nrow(df_cluster), "rows,",
    dplyr::n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c), "countries.\n")
print(dplyr::count(df_cluster, km_cluster))
```


### Cluster visualisation in PCA space (reference year)

```{r cluster-pca-scatter, fig.height=6}
ggplot(df_cluster, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = km_cluster, shape = Development_Tier, label = iso3c)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 30) +
  labs(title = paste("K-means clusters in PCA space — reference year", REF_YEAR),
       x = sprintf("PC1 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[1]),
       y = sprintf("PC2 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[2]),
       colour = "Cluster", shape = "Tier") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Cluster profiles in original variable space

To interpret clusters substantively, we summarise each cluster by the mean **standardised** value (z-score) of each original indicator in the reference year. This directly answers: *what variables define each cluster?*

```{r cluster-profiles}
# Standardise within the reference-year cross-section for interpretation
df_prof <- df_cluster |>
  mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), z))

profile_tbl <- df_prof |>
  group_by(km_cluster) |>
  summarise(across(all_of(analysis_vars), ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)), .groups = "drop")

profile_tbl |>
  kable(digits = 2, caption = "Cluster profiles (mean z-scores) — higher = above-sample average") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r cluster-profile-heatmap, fig.height=6}
profile_long <- profile_tbl |>
  pivot_longer(-km_cluster, names_to = "variable", values_to = "z_mean")

ggplot(profile_long, aes(variable, km_cluster, fill = z_mean)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "tomato", mid = "white", high = "steelblue", midpoint = 0) +
  labs(title = paste("Cluster profile heatmap (z-means) — reference year", REF_YEAR),
       x = NULL, y = "K-means cluster", fill = "Mean z") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```


### Per-Observation Silhouette

```{r silhouette-obs, fig.height=6}
sil_obs <- silhouette(km_fit$cluster, dist(pca_space))
sil_df  <- as.data.frame(sil_obs[, 1:3])
names(sil_df) <- c("cluster", "neighbor", "sil_width")
sil_df$cluster <- factor(sil_df$cluster)

# Summary stats per cluster
sil_summary <- sil_df |>
  group_by(cluster) |>
  summarise(
    n       = n(),
    avg_sil = mean(sil_width),
    neg_pct = mean(sil_width < 0) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

overall_avg <- mean(sil_df$sil_width)
cat("Overall average silhouette width:", round(overall_avg, 3), "\n")
print(sil_summary)

# Left panel: average silhouette per cluster
p_bar <- ggplot(sil_summary, aes(cluster, avg_sil, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, alpha = 0.88) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = overall_avg, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("n = %d\navg = %.2f", n, avg_sil)),
            vjust = -0.35, size = 3.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = -Inf, y = overall_avg,
           label = sprintf("Overall = %.2f", overall_avg),
           hjust = -0.1, vjust = -0.5, colour = "red", size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, max(sil_summary$avg_sil) * 1.3),
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.1)) +
  labs(title = "Average Silhouette Width by Cluster",
       subtitle = "Red line = overall average | Higher = tighter, better-separated cluster",
       x = "Cluster", y = "Avg Silhouette Width") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Right panel: within-cluster distribution of silhouette widths
p_box <- ggplot(sil_df, aes(cluster, sil_width, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.55, trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.size = 0.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  labs(title = "Silhouette Width Distribution Within Each Cluster",
       subtitle = "Values below 0 indicate potentially misassigned observations",
       x = "Cluster", y = "Silhouette Width") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_bar, p_box, ncol = 2)
```

## 5.3 Hierarchical Clustering

**Hierarchical agglomerative clustering** builds a tree-like structure (dendrogram) by iteratively merging the two closest clusters, starting from individual observations. The full merge history is then cut at $k$ = `r k_final` groups.

We use **Ward's D2 linkage**, which minimises the total within-cluster variance at each merge step, producing compact clusters that are most comparable to the K-Means solution. Ward's D2 is the standard choice when comparing hierarchical results to K-Means, and it consistently produces clusters that are more interpretable and balanced than single or complete linkage in economic data.

```{r hclust-methods}
# Single hclust object with country-labelled rows for both dendrogram and assignments
pca_mat <- df_cluster |>
  select(iso3c, all_of(paste0("PC", 1:n_pcs))) |>
  as.data.frame()
rownames(pca_mat) <- pca_mat$iso3c
pca_mat <- pca_mat[, -1, drop = FALSE]

d <- dist(pca_mat, method = "euclidean")
hc_ward <- hclust(d, method = "ward.D2")

coph_cor <- round(cor(d, cophenetic(hc_ward)), 3)
cat("Ward.D2 cophenetic correlation:", coph_cor, "\n")
cat("Interpretation:", ifelse(coph_cor >= 0.75, "good — dendrogram faithfully represents distances (Sokal & Rohlf threshold: 0.75)",
    "moderate — some distortion in dendrogram representation"), "\n")
```

### Dendrogram

Height on the y-axis represents the distance at which clusters were merged. Large jumps indicate structural breaks in the data — points where merging is costly because genuinely dissimilar groups are being combined. The rectangles show the `r k_final` clusters obtained by cutting the tree.

```{r dendrogram, fig.height=7, fig.width=14}
dend <- as.dendrogram(hc_ward) |>
  set("labels_cex", 0.55) |>
  color_branches(k = k_final)

plot(dend,
     main = paste("Hierarchical Dendrogram (Ward.D2) — Reference Year", REF_YEAR, "| k =", k_final),
     ylab = "Height")
rect.hclust(hc_ward, k = k_final, border = 2:(k_final + 1))
```

```{r hclust-assign}
hc_clusters  <- cutree(hc_ward, k = k_final)
df_cluster$hc_cluster <- factor(hc_clusters)
```

### Linkage Sensitivity Check

To verify that Ward.D2's cluster assignments are not an artefact of the specific linkage rule, we compare the Ward solution against average and complete linkage using ARI. High agreement indicates that the cluster structure is an intrinsic feature of the data, not a function of the linkage criterion. (item 23)

```{r linkage-sensitivity}
hc_avg  <- hclust(d, method = "average")
hc_comp <- hclust(d, method = "complete")

cl_ward <- cutree(hc_ward, k = k_final)
cl_avg  <- cutree(hc_avg,  k = k_final)
cl_comp <- cutree(hc_comp, k = k_final)

ari_ward_avg  <- adjustedRandIndex(cl_ward, cl_avg)
ari_ward_comp <- adjustedRandIndex(cl_ward, cl_comp)

tibble(
  Comparison     = c("Ward.D2 vs Average", "Ward.D2 vs Complete"),
  ARI            = c(ari_ward_avg, ari_ward_comp),
  Interpretation = case_when(
    ARI >= 0.90 ~ "Near-identical",
    ARI >= 0.75 ~ "Strong agreement",
    ARI >= 0.60 ~ "Moderate agreement",
    TRUE        ~ "Substantial divergence"
  )
) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = "Hierarchical Clustering: Ward.D2 vs Alternative Linkages") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## 5.4 Model-Based Clustering (Gaussian Mixture)

**Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)** assume the data is generated by a mixture of multivariate normal distributions, each corresponding to a cluster. The Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) simultaneously selects the optimal number of clusters and the best covariance model (spherical, ellipsoidal, equal/varying size and orientation). GMMs additionally provide **soft classification** — each observation receives a probability of belonging to each cluster, along with an uncertainty score. Mardia's test (Section 4.1) assesses the multivariate normality assumption underlying this method.

```{r mclust, fig.height=6}
mc_fit <- Mclust(as.data.frame(pca_space))

cat("Best GMM model:", mc_fit$modelName,
    "| k =", mc_fit$G,
    "| BIC =", round(mc_fit$bic, 1), "\n")

plot(mc_fit, what = "BIC",
     main = "BIC by Model Type and Number of Components")
```

```{r mclust-plot, fig.height=7}
plot(mc_fit, what = "classification",
     main = paste("Model-Based Clusters (", mc_fit$modelName, ", k =", mc_fit$G, ")"))
```

```{r mclust-assign}
df_cluster$mc_cluster   <- factor(mc_fit$classification)
df_cluster$mc_uncertainty <- mc_fit$uncertainty

cat("Observations with classification uncertainty > 0.1:",
    sum(mc_fit$uncertainty > 0.1), "of", nrow(pca_space), "\n")
```

```{r make-df_clustered}
# Create df_clustered AFTER all three clustering methods have been applied
# so it contains km_cluster, hc_cluster, and mc_cluster
df_clustered <- df_cluster
stopifnot(all(c("km_cluster", "hc_cluster", "mc_cluster") %in% names(df_clustered)))
```

**Note on $k$:** The GMM selected **`r mc_fit$G` clusters** with a `r mc_fit$modelName` covariance structure via BIC. `r if (mc_fit$G == k_final) "This independently confirms our NbClust-based selection —the GMM's data-driven model selection agrees with the majority vote." else paste0("This differs from the K-Means/hierarchical k = ", k_final, ", reflecting the GMM's greater flexibility in accommodating non-spherical cluster shapes. The cross-method comparison below uses each method's own cluster labels.")`

## 5.5 Method Comparison

High agreement across three very different algorithms —K-Means (distance-based, spherical), Hierarchical (tree-based, Ward linkage), and GMM (probabilistic, flexible covariance) —is a strong signal that the cluster structure reflects genuine patterns in the data. We quantify agreement using the **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)**, which measures pairwise agreement between clusterings corrected for chance. ARI = 1.0 indicates perfect agreement; ARI = 0 indicates chance-level agreement.

```{r cross-tab}
cross_km_hc <- table(KMeans = df_cluster$km_cluster, Hierarchical = df_cluster$hc_cluster)
cross_km_mc <- table(KMeans = df_cluster$km_cluster, ModelBased   = df_cluster$mc_cluster)

cat("K-Means vs Hierarchical:\n"); print(cross_km_hc)
cat("\nK-Means vs Model-Based:\n");  print(cross_km_mc)
```

```{r ari-comparison}
ari_km_hc <- adjustedRandIndex(df_cluster$km_cluster, df_cluster$hc_cluster)
ari_km_mc <- adjustedRandIndex(df_cluster$km_cluster, df_cluster$mc_cluster)
ari_hc_mc <- adjustedRandIndex(df_cluster$hc_cluster, df_cluster$mc_cluster)

tibble(
  Pair = c("K-Means vs Hierarchical",
           "K-Means vs Model-Based",
           "Hierarchical vs Model-Based"),
  ARI  = c(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc)
) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = "Adjusted Rand Index: Cross-Method Agreement (ARI > 0.8 = strong; > 0.65 = moderate)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

### Alluvial Diagram: Country Flow Across Methods

The alluvial diagram below visualises how individual countries are assigned by each of the three clustering methods. Thick, unbroken flows indicate consensus; fragmented flows reveal countries where methods disagree — the structural "borderline" cases.

```{r alluvial, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
alluvial_df <- df_cluster |>
  select(iso3c, km_cluster, hc_cluster, mc_cluster) |>
  rename(`K-Means` = km_cluster, Hierarchical = hc_cluster, `GMM` = mc_cluster) |>
  pivot_longer(-iso3c, names_to = "Method", values_to = "Cluster") |>
  mutate(Method = factor(Method, levels = c("K-Means", "Hierarchical", "GMM")))

ggplot(alluvial_df,
       aes(x = Method, stratum = Cluster, alluvium = iso3c, fill = Cluster)) +
  geom_flow(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_stratum(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum)), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  labs(title = "Country Assignment Flow Across Three Clustering Methods",
       subtitle = "Each ribbon tracks one country across methods; fragmented flows = borderline cases",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

### Stability under resampling (subsample ARI)

A good clustering should be reasonably stable to modest perturbations of the sample. We subsample countries (without replacement), refit k-means, then compare the induced partition on the overlapping countries to the original solution using ARI.

```{r cluster-stability, fig.height=5, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

set.seed(123)
B <- 50
keep_frac <- 0.8

orig_assign <- df_cluster |> dplyr::select(iso3c, km_cluster)

ari_boot <- purrr::map_dbl(seq_len(B), function(b) {
  keep <- sample(df_cluster$iso3c,
                 size = floor(keep_frac * nrow(df_cluster)),
                 replace = FALSE)

  df_b  <- df_cluster |> dplyr::filter(iso3c %in% keep)
  pca_b <- df_b |> dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(paste0("PC", 1:n_pcs)))

  km_b  <- kmeans(pca_b, centers = k_final, nstart = 50)
  ass_b <- tibble(iso3c = df_b$iso3c, km_b = factor(km_b$cluster))

  joined <- dplyr::inner_join(orig_assign, ass_b, by = "iso3c")
  mclust::adjustedRandIndex(joined$km_cluster, joined$km_b)
})

ari_df <- tibble(ARI = ari_boot)

mu  <- mean(ari_boot, na.rm = TRUE)
med <- median(ari_boot, na.rm = TRUE)
p10 <- as.numeric(stats::quantile(ari_boot, 0.10, na.rm = TRUE))
p90 <- as.numeric(stats::quantile(ari_boot, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE))

# Colorblind-safe palette (Okabe–Ito)
col_hist <- "#4D4D4D"   # neutral dark grey
col_den  <- "#0072B2"   # blue
col_mean <- "#D55E00"   # vermillion
col_med  <- "#009E73"   # bluish green
band_weak <- "#D55E00"  # weak (warm)
band_mod  <- "#E69F00"  # moderate (amber)
band_str  <- "#009E73"  # strong (green)

ggplot(ari_df, aes(ARI)) +
  # interpretation bands (now colored, but subtle + accessible)
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = 0.75, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = band_weak, alpha = 0.08) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.75, xmax = 0.90, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = band_mod, alpha = 0.07) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.90, xmax = Inf, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = band_str, alpha = 0.06) +

  geom_histogram(bins = 18, boundary = 0, closed = "left",
                 fill = col_hist, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.90) +
  geom_density(colour = col_den, linewidth = 1.1, adjust = 1.1) +
  geom_rug(colour = col_hist, alpha = 0.18) +

  geom_vline(xintercept = mu,  linetype = "dashed",  colour = col_mean, linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = med, linetype = "dotdash", colour = col_med,  linewidth = 1.0) +

  annotate(
    "label",
    x = 0.55, y = Inf, hjust = 0, vjust = 1.15,
    label = sprintf(
      "B=%d, keep=%.0f%%\nMean=%.3f | Median=%.3f\n10–90%%: %.3f–%.3f",
      B, keep_frac*100, mu, med, p10, p90
    ),
    label.size = 0.25,
    fill = "white",
    colour = "black"
  ) +

  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.1)) +
  labs(
    title = "K-means stability under 80% subsampling (ARI distribution)",
    subtitle = "Bands: weak (<0.75), moderate (0.75–0.90), strong (≥0.90). Dashed=mean, dotdash=median.",
    x = "Adjusted Rand Index vs original clustering",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

stability_label <- ifelse(mu >= 0.90, "highly stable",
                          ifelse(mu >= 0.75, "moderately stable", "unstable"))
knitr::asis_output(sprintf(
  "<div class='callout callout-info'><strong>Subsample stability:</strong> mean ARI = %.3f (median %.3f), 10–90%% range %.3f–%.3f — clustering is <strong>%s</strong> under 80%% subsampling.</div>",
  mu, med, p10, p90, stability_label
))
```


## 5.6 Cluster Stability (K-Means)

We re-run K-Means with five different random seeds and compute ARI between every pair of resulting clusterings. ARI = 1.0 across all pairs indicates that the cluster assignments are perfectly reproducible regardless of initialisation.

```{r stability}
seeds <- c(1, 7, 42, 99, 202)
cl_list <- purrr::map(seeds, \(s) {
  set.seed(s); kmeans(pca_space, k_final, nstart = 100)$cluster
})

ari_stability <- expand_grid(i = seq_along(seeds), j = seq_along(seeds)) |>
  filter(i < j) |>
  mutate(ARI = purrr::map2_dbl(i, j,
                                \(a, b) adjustedRandIndex(cl_list[[a]], cl_list[[b]])))

seed_ari_min <- round(min(ari_stability$ARI), 3)
seed_ari_max <- round(max(ari_stability$ARI), 3)
seed_label   <- ifelse(seed_ari_min >= 0.99, "perfectly stable",
                       ifelse(seed_ari_min >= 0.85, "highly stable", "moderately stable"))
knitr::asis_output(sprintf(
  "<div class='callout callout-info'><strong>Seed stability:</strong> ARI range %.3f–%.3f across 5 random initialisations — assignments are <strong>%s</strong> and not an artefact of initialisation.</div>",
  seed_ari_min, seed_ari_max, seed_label
))
```

### PCA-Space vs. Raw-Variable Clustering Comparison

A key methodological question: does clustering in PCA space produce meaningfully different results from clustering on the raw (scaled) variables? If the two approaches agree strongly, PCA-based clustering is validated as a dimensionality reduction step that preserves the clustering signal. If they diverge, important structure may be lost in the PCA projection.

```{r pca-vs-raw}
# Cluster on raw scaled variables (same k, same algorithm)
raw_scaled <- scale(df_cluster[, analysis_vars])
set.seed(42)
km_raw <- kmeans(raw_scaled, centers = k_final, nstart = 100)

# Compare with PCA-based clustering
ari_pca_raw <- adjustedRandIndex(km_fit$cluster, km_raw$cluster)

# Silhouette comparison
sil_pca <- mean(silhouette(km_fit$cluster, dist(pca_space))[, "sil_width"])
sil_raw <- mean(silhouette(km_raw$cluster, dist(raw_scaled))[, "sil_width"])

tibble(
  Metric = c("ARI (PCA vs. raw)", "Mean silhouette (PCA space)", "Mean silhouette (raw space)"),
  Value = c(round(ari_pca_raw, 3), round(sil_pca, 3), round(sil_raw, 3))
) |>
  kable(caption = "PCA-Space vs. Raw-Variable Clustering Comparison") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

ari_label <- ifelse(ari_pca_raw >= 0.90, "near-identical",
             ifelse(ari_pca_raw >= 0.75, "strongly similar",
             ifelse(ari_pca_raw >= 0.50, "moderately similar", "notably different")))
```

<div class="callout callout-info">
**PCA vs. raw clustering:** ARI = `r round(ari_pca_raw, 3)` — the two approaches produce `r ari_label` partitions. `r if(sil_pca >= sil_raw) "PCA-space clustering achieves equal or higher silhouette, confirming that dimensionality reduction preserves (or sharpens) the clustering signal." else "Raw-space clustering achieves marginally higher silhouette, suggesting that some separating variance resides in minor PCA components. The practical difference is small."`
</div>

## 5.7 Cluster Profiles
