We profile each cluster on the original (unstandardised) analysis variables and their z-scored means. Positive z-scores indicate the cluster is above the global average on that variable; negative z-scores indicate below.

```{r profiles-z}


stopifnot("km_cluster" %in% names(df_cluster))

cluster_z <- df_cluster |>
  dplyr::select(km_cluster, dplyr::all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(-km_cluster, z)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
  dplyr::summarise(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
                   .groups = "drop")

cluster_z |>
  kable(digits = 2,
        caption = "Cluster Profiles (z-scored means; positive = above sample average)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)

```

```{r profiles-raw}

if (!("km_cluster" %in% names(df_cluster))) {
  stop("km_cluster not found in df_cluster. Ensure the {r kmeans} chunk ran before this chunk.", call. = FALSE)
}

cluster_raw <- df_cluster |>
  dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(analysis_vars), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
    n = dplyr::n(),
    n_countries = dplyr::n_distinct(iso3c),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cluster_raw |>
  kable(digits = 2, caption = "Cluster Profiles (raw means, reference-year sample)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Radar Chart

```{r radar, fig.height=7, fig.width=9}
radar_data <- cluster_z |>
  pivot_longer(-km_cluster, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "z_score")

ggplot(radar_data, aes(x = Variable, y = z_score,
                        group = km_cluster, colour = km_cluster, fill = km_cluster)) +
  geom_polygon(alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  coord_polar() +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", name = "Cluster") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D", name = "Cluster") +
  labs(title = "Cluster Profiles —z-scored Mean on Each Analysis Variable",
       x = NULL, y = "z-score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))
```

### Boxplots by Cluster

```{r boxplots, fig.height=11}

df_clustered |>
  dplyr::select(km_cluster, dplyr::all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-km_cluster, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") |>
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(km_cluster, Value, fill = km_cluster)) +
  ggplot2::geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ Variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Variable Distributions by Cluster (reference-year sample)",
                x = "Cluster", fill = "Cluster") +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()
```

### Parallel Coordinates Plot

Parallel coordinates display each country as a line traversing all eleven analysis variables (z-scored), coloured by cluster assignment. This reveals which variables most sharply separate clusters and exposes individual countries whose profiles deviate from their cluster's central tendency.

```{r parallel-coords, fig.height=8, fig.width=12}
pc_df <- df_clustered |>
  mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), z)) |>
  select(iso3c, km_cluster, all_of(analysis_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(analysis_vars), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "z_value")

ggplot(pc_df, aes(x = Variable, y = z_value, group = iso3c, colour = km_cluster)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.4) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = km_cluster), fun = mean, geom = "line",
               linewidth = 1.4, alpha = 1) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = km_cluster), fun = mean, geom = "point",
               size = 2.5, alpha = 1) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", name = "Cluster") +
  labs(title = "Parallel Coordinates — All Analysis Variables by Cluster",
       subtitle = "Thin lines = individual countries; thick lines = cluster means (z-scored)",
       x = NULL, y = "z-score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
        legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Prototype Countries (Closest to Cluster Centroids)

For each cluster, the five countries with the shortest Euclidean distance to their cluster centroid in PCA space are the most representative members — the structural "archetypes" of that group. (item 25)

```{r prototypes}
# Compute cluster centroids in PCA space
centroids <- as.data.frame(pca_space) |>
  mutate(cluster = km_fit$cluster) |>
  group_by(cluster) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), mean), .groups = "drop")

proto_df <- purrr::map_dfr(sort(unique(km_fit$cluster)), function(cl) {
  cl_idx    <- which(km_fit$cluster == cl)
  cl_data   <- as.matrix(pca_space[cl_idx, ])
  centroid  <- centroids |> filter(cluster == cl) |> select(-cluster) |> as.numeric()
  dists     <- apply(cl_data, 1, function(r) sqrt(sum((r - centroid)^2)))
  top_idx   <- cl_idx[order(dists)[seq_len(min(5, length(cl_idx)))]]
  df_cluster[top_idx, c("iso3c", "country", "Development_Tier")] |>
    mutate(Cluster = cl, Dist_to_Centroid = sort(dists)[seq_len(min(5, length(cl_idx)))])
})

proto_df |>
  arrange(Cluster, Dist_to_Centroid) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        col.names = c("ISO3", "Country", "Tier", "Cluster", "Distance to Centroid"),
        caption = "Five Prototype Countries Per Cluster (Closest to Centroid in PCA Space)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE) |>
  collapse_rows(columns = 4, valign = "top")
```

### Cluster Composition by Development Tier

A critical output for this global analysis: to what extent do the data-driven clusters align with the World Bank income classification? High alignment confirms that our unsupervised method has recovered the global development hierarchy; systematic divergence would reveal finer structural distinctions beyond what income level alone captures.

```{r cluster-tier-tab}

cluster_tier <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::count(km_cluster, Development_Tier) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Development_Tier, values_from = n, values_fill = 0L)

cluster_tier |>
  kable(caption = paste0("Cluster Composition by Development Tier (Reference Year: ", REF_YEAR, ")")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```


```{r cluster-tier-plotv, fig.height=5 }

tier_pct <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::mutate(km_cluster = factor(km_cluster, levels = sort(unique(km_cluster)))) |>
  dplyr::count(km_cluster, Development_Tier, name = "n") |>
  dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
  dplyr::mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Development_Tier = factor(
      Development_Tier,
      levels = c("Advanced Economies", "Emerging Economies", "Developing Economies")
    )
  )

ggplot(tier_pct, aes(km_cluster, pct, fill = Development_Tier)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.62, alpha = 0.92) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(pct >= 0.06, sprintf("%d\n(%.0f%%)", n, pct * 100), "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.4, fontface = "bold", colour = "white", lineheight = 0.95
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = tier_pal, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("Development Tier Composition Within Each Cluster (", REF_YEAR, ")"),
    subtitle = "Labels show country count and share of cluster; segments < 6% unlabelled",
    x = "Cluster", y = "Share of cluster", fill = "Development tier"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )
```


```{r tier-cluster-test, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}

tab <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::count(km_cluster, Development_Tier) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Development_Tier, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  tibble::column_to_rownames("km_cluster") |>
  as.matrix()

# Fisher’s exact test (robust to low expected cell counts in small contingency tables)
fisher_res <- fisher.test(tab, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 10000)

# Also compute Cramér’s V effect size (0–1)
chi <- chisq.test(tab)
n_tot <- sum(tab)
k_min <- min(nrow(tab) - 1, ncol(tab) - 1)
cramers_v <- sqrt(as.numeric(chi$statistic) / (n_tot * k_min))

knitr::asis_output(sprintf(
  "<div class=’callout callout-info’><strong>Association test:</strong> Fisher’s exact p=%s (10,000 simulations); Cramér’s V=%.2f.</div>",
  format.pval(fisher_res$p.value, digits=3), cramers_v
))
```


## 5.8 External Validation: Oxford AI Readiness Index (All Pillars)

The Kaggle automation rates and Oxford Insights scores were excluded from the PCA/clustering pipeline (Section 3.3). We now use them as **independent external validators**: if our structurally derived clusters show meaningful differentiation on these AI-specific metrics, it confirms that the clusters capture genuine differences in AI preparedness — not artefacts of variable selection.

The Oxford Insights index is a 2025 cross-section. It is used here as a **slow-moving institutional capacity proxy** rather than a time-matched observation — government digital readiness, infrastructure, and policy capacity evolve on decade-long timescales, so the 2025 scores are informative for clusters defined at the reference year.

```{r external-validation-kaggle}
# Kaggle synthetic metrics (illustrative only)
# Pull from the merged panel (all_data) since df_pca is now cross-section only
kaggle_ref <- all_data |>
  dplyr::filter(year == REF_YEAR) |>
  dplyr::select(iso3c, automation_rate, ai_readiness_score)

kaggle_val <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::select(iso3c, country, km_cluster, Development_Tier) |>
  dplyr::left_join(kaggle_ref, by = "iso3c") |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(automation_rate) | !is.na(ai_readiness_score))

if (nrow(kaggle_val) > 0) {
  kaggle_val |>
    dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n = dplyr::n(),
      Automation_Rate    = mean(automation_rate,    na.rm = TRUE),
      AI_Readiness_Score = mean(ai_readiness_score, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    kable(digits = 2,
          col.names = c("Cluster", "n", "Automation Rate (%)", "AI Readiness (Kaggle, synthetic)"),
          caption = paste0("External Validation: Kaggle Synthetic AI Metrics by Cluster (", REF_YEAR, ")")) |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
} else {
  cat("Kaggle data not available for the reference year — skipped.\n")
}
```

```{r external-validation-oxford}
# Oxford AI Readiness — all available pillars (item 26)
ox_pillar_cols <- intersect(
  c("overall_score", "policy_capacity", "ai_infrastructure",
    "governance", "public_sector_adoption", "development_diffusion", "resilience"),
  names(ai_readiness)
)

oxford_val <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::select(iso3c, country, km_cluster, Development_Tier) |>
  dplyr::left_join(ai_readiness |> dplyr::select(iso3c, dplyr::all_of(ox_pillar_cols)),
                   by = "iso3c")

if (length(ox_pillar_cols) > 0 && any(!is.na(oxford_val$overall_score))) {

  # Table: cluster means across all pillars
  oxford_val |>
    dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n = dplyr::n(),
      dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(ox_pillar_cols), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    kable(digits = 1,
          caption = paste0(
            "External Validation: Oxford AI Readiness — Cluster Means on All Pillars\n",
            "(Oxford Insights 2025; treated as slow-moving institutional capacity proxy)")) |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
    print()

  # Heatmap: pillar z-scores by cluster
  ox_z <- oxford_val |>
    dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
    dplyr::summarise(dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(ox_pillar_cols),
                                   ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)), .groups = "drop") |>
    tidyr::pivot_longer(-km_cluster, names_to = "Pillar", values_to = "Mean_Score") |>
    dplyr::group_by(Pillar) |>
    dplyr::mutate(z_mean = as.numeric(scale(Mean_Score))) |>
    dplyr::ungroup()

  p_ox_heat <- ggplot(ox_z, aes(Pillar, km_cluster, fill = z_mean)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#D55E00", mid = "white", high = "#0072B2", midpoint = 0,
                         name = "z-score") +
    labs(title = "Oxford AI Readiness Pillars by Cluster (z-standardised across clusters)",
         subtitle = "Blue = above average; Red = below average",
         x = NULL, y = "Cluster") +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
  print(p_ox_heat)

  # Boxplot: overall score by cluster
  p_ox_box <- oxford_val |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(overall_score)) |>
    ggplot(aes(km_cluster, overall_score, fill = km_cluster)) +
    geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D") +
    labs(title = "Oxford AI Readiness Overall Score by Cluster",
         x = "Cluster", y = "Overall Score (0–100)") +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
  print(p_ox_box)

  cat("Oxford coverage:", sum(!is.na(oxford_val$overall_score)), "of",
      nrow(oxford_val), "clustered countries.\n")

} else {
  cat("Oxford AI Readiness data not available — external validation skipped.\n")
}
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 6. Vulnerability Index & Rankings

Clustering identifies *groups* of structurally similar countries but does not directly quantify *how vulnerable* each country is. To answer our core research question, we construct a composite **Vulnerability Index** that synthesises each country's structural profile into a single interpretable score.

The index rests on a theoretically motivated two-part framework:

**Exposure** measures the degree to which a country's economy is concentrated in activities most susceptible to AI automation. We operationalise it as the *average z-score* of three exposure indicators:

$$\text{Exposure} = \frac{z(\text{Agric\_Empl}) + z(\text{Industry\_Empl}) + z(\text{Unemp\_Youth})}{3}$$

Agricultural employment captures dependence on a sector with high manual-task intensity and low digitalisation. Industrial employment captures the share in manufacturing and related sectors where robotics and AI-driven automation are most advanced. Youth unemployment signals weak labour market absorption capacity — young people who cannot enter productive employment today face the greatest structural displacement risk tomorrow.

**Resilience** captures the structural buffers that allow a country to adapt, retrain workers, and capture the productivity gains that AI offers. We operationalise it as the *average z-score* of five resilience indicators:

$$\text{Resilience} = \frac{z(\text{GDP\_pc}) + z(\text{RD\_Intensity}) + z(\text{Tertiary\_Edu}) + z(\text{Internet\_Users}) + z(\text{Labor\_Part})}{5}$$

The **Vulnerability Index** is:

$$\text{Vulnerability} = \text{Exposure} - \text{Resilience}$$

By averaging z-scores *within* each group, both Exposure and Resilience are expressed on the same mean-z-score scale (approximately −1 to +1), ensuring that neither group dominates the composite index due to having more components. Positive Vulnerability scores indicate net exposure outweighing resilience; negative values indicate net resilience.

**Note on excluded analysis variables:** Two variables included in the PCA — `HiTech_Exports` and `GDP_Growth` — are deliberately excluded from the Vulnerability Index. `HiTech_Exports` is a trade-structure descriptor that partially overlaps with `Industry_Empl` and `RD_Intensity`; including it would introduce redundancy into the exposure-resilience balance. `GDP_Growth` is a *cyclical* indicator (it reflects the most recent business-cycle position, not a country's structural capacity), and vulnerability is inherently a structural, medium-to-long-run concept. Including cyclical measures would cause the index to penalise countries experiencing temporary economic slowdowns regardless of their structural fundamentals.

```{r vulnerability}
# z-scores computed on the reference-year cross-section (df_pca = df_ref + PCA scores)
# so standardization reflects cross-country variation only, not temporal variation.
df_pca <- df_pca |>
  mutate(
    Exposure = (z(Agric_Empl) + z(Industry_Empl) + z(Unemp_Youth)) / 3,
    Resilience = (z(GDP_pc) + z(RD_Intensity) + z(Tertiary_Edu) +
                  z(Internet_Users) + z(Labor_Part)) / 5,
    Vulnerability = Exposure - Resilience
  )
```

```{r vulnerability-rankings, fig.height=14, fig.width=10}
# df_pca is already the reference-year cross-section (one row per country)
rank_vuln <- df_pca |>
  select(iso3c, country, Development_Tier, Vulnerability, Exposure, Resilience) |>
  left_join(df_cluster |> select(iso3c, km_cluster), by = "iso3c") |>
  arrange(desc(Vulnerability))

ggplot(rank_vuln, aes(Vulnerability, reorder(country, Vulnerability),
                       fill = Development_Tier)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  labs(
    title = "Global AI Vulnerability Index Rankings",
    subtitle = "Most recent available year per country | Positive = more vulnerable | Negative = more resilient",
    x = "Vulnerability Index (mean Exposure z-score − mean Resilience z-score)",
    y = NULL, fill = "Development Tier"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))
```

### Top and Bottom Countries

```{r top-bottom, results='asis'}
tbl_cols <- c(Country = "country", Tier = "Development_Tier",
              Cluster = "km_cluster",
              Vulnerability = "Vulnerability",
              Exposure = "Exposure", Resilience = "Resilience")

cat("\n#### Top 10 Most Vulnerable Countries\n\n")
rank_vuln |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(all_of(tbl_cols)) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = paste("Most exposed to AI-driven disruption —", REF_YEAR)) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  print()

cat("\n#### Top 10 Most Resilient Countries\n\n")
rank_vuln |>
  slice_tail(n = 10) |>
  arrange(Vulnerability) |>         # most resilient (most negative) first
  select(all_of(tbl_cols)) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = paste("Strongest structural buffers against AI disruption —", REF_YEAR)) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  print()
```

### Mean Vulnerability by Development Tier

```{r vuln-by-tier}
rank_vuln |>
  group_by(Development_Tier) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    Mean_Vulnerability = mean(Vulnerability),
    Mean_Exposure      = mean(Exposure),
    Mean_Resilience    = mean(Resilience),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = "Mean Vulnerability Components by Development Tier") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Formal Tests of Vulnerability Differences

```{r vuln-tests}
# Kruskal-Wallis test: vulnerability differs across clusters?
kw_cluster <- kruskal.test(Vulnerability ~ km_cluster, data = rank_vuln)
cat("Kruskal-Wallis (Vulnerability ~ Cluster): H =", round(kw_cluster$statistic, 2),
    ", df =", kw_cluster$parameter, ", p =", format.pval(kw_cluster$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")

# Kruskal-Wallis test: vulnerability differs across development tiers?
kw_tier <- kruskal.test(Vulnerability ~ Development_Tier, data = rank_vuln)
cat("Kruskal-Wallis (Vulnerability ~ Tier): H =", round(kw_tier$statistic, 2),
    ", df =", kw_tier$parameter, ", p =", format.pval(kw_tier$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")

# Post-hoc pairwise Wilcoxon with Holm correction
cat("\nPost-hoc pairwise Wilcoxon (Vulnerability ~ Tier, Holm-adjusted):\n")
pairwise.wilcox.test(rank_vuln$Vulnerability, rank_vuln$Development_Tier,
                     p.adjust.method = "holm") |> print()
```

### Exposure vs. Resilience Scatter

This scatter plot is the most informative single visualisation of the vulnerability framework. Countries above the diagonal (Exposure > Resilience) are structurally vulnerable; those below are structurally resilient. The distance from the diagonal is the Vulnerability Index score.

```{r exposure-resilience, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
ggplot(rank_vuln, aes(Resilience, Exposure, colour = Development_Tier, label = iso3c)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 25) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  annotate("text", x = -1.2, y = 1.0, label = "High Exposure\nLow Resilience\n(Most Vulnerable)",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "tomato", fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = -0.8, label = "Low Exposure\nHigh Resilience\n(Most Resilient)",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "steelblue", fontface = "italic") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Exposure vs. Resilience — Reference Year", REF_YEAR),
    subtitle = "Diagonal = zero vulnerability; above = net exposed, below = net resilient",
    x = "Resilience (mean z-score of GDP pc, R&D, Tertiary Edu, Internet, Labor Part)",
    y = "Exposure (mean z-score of Agric Empl, Industry Empl, Youth Unemp)",
    colour = "Development Tier"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Vulnerability Index Sensitivity Analysis

Equal weighting is a transparent but potentially influential choice. We test ranking stability across four alternative specifications: (1) **PCA-weighted** — using PC1 loadings as variable weights; (2) **leave-one-out** — removing each variable in turn; (3) **double R&D weight**; and (4) **raw PC1 score** as a standalone proxy.

```{r sensitivity, fig.height=6}
# Baseline ranking for comparison
baseline_rank <- rank_vuln |> mutate(rank_base = row_number()) |> select(iso3c, rank_base)

# Get PC1 loadings for PCA-weighted specification
pc1_loads <- get_pca_var(pca_res)$coord[, 1]
exposure_vars  <- c("Agric_Empl", "Industry_Empl", "Unemp_Youth")
resilience_vars <- c("GDP_pc", "RD_Intensity", "Tertiary_Edu", "Internet_Users", "Labor_Part")

# Spec 1: PCA-weighted vulnerability
exp_weights <- abs(pc1_loads[exposure_vars]) / sum(abs(pc1_loads[exposure_vars]))
res_weights <- abs(pc1_loads[resilience_vars]) / sum(abs(pc1_loads[resilience_vars]))

vuln_pca_wt <- df_pca |>
  mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), z)) |>
  mutate(
    E_pca = rowSums(across(all_of(exposure_vars)) * matrix(exp_weights, nrow = n(), ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)),
    R_pca = rowSums(across(all_of(resilience_vars)) * matrix(res_weights, nrow = n(), ncol = 5, byrow = TRUE)),
    V_pca = E_pca - R_pca
  ) |>
  select(iso3c, V_pca) |>
  arrange(desc(V_pca)) |>
  mutate(rank_pca = row_number())

# Spec 2: Double weight on R&D
vuln_dbl_rd <- df_pca |>
  mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), z)) |>
  mutate(
    R_dbl = (GDP_pc + 2*RD_Intensity + Tertiary_Edu + Internet_Users + Labor_Part) / 6,
    V_dbl = (Agric_Empl + Industry_Empl + Unemp_Youth) / 3 - R_dbl
  ) |>
  select(iso3c, V_dbl) |>
  arrange(desc(V_dbl)) |>
  mutate(rank_dbl = row_number())

# Spec 3: Raw PC1 as vulnerability proxy (reversed: low PC1 = more vulnerable)
vuln_pc1 <- df_pca |>
  select(iso3c, PC1) |>
  arrange(PC1) |>
  mutate(rank_pc1 = row_number())

# Combine and compute Spearman correlations
sens_df <- baseline_rank |>
  left_join(vuln_pca_wt |> select(iso3c, rank_pca), by = "iso3c") |>
  left_join(vuln_dbl_rd |> select(iso3c, rank_dbl), by = "iso3c") |>
  left_join(vuln_pc1 |> select(iso3c, rank_pc1), by = "iso3c")

sens_cors <- tibble(
  Specification = c("PCA-weighted", "Double R&D weight", "Raw PC1 (reversed)"),
  Spearman_rho = c(
    cor(sens_df$rank_base, sens_df$rank_pca, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs"),
    cor(sens_df$rank_base, sens_df$rank_dbl, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs"),
    cor(sens_df$rank_base, sens_df$rank_pc1, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs")
  )
)

sens_cors |>
  kable(digits = 3,
        caption = "Vulnerability Ranking Sensitivity: Spearman Rank Correlation with Baseline") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

# Visualise rank stability
sens_long <- sens_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(rank_pca, rank_dbl, rank_pc1), names_to = "spec", values_to = "alt_rank") |>
  mutate(spec = recode(spec, rank_pca = "PCA-weighted", rank_dbl = "Double R&D", rank_pc1 = "Raw PC1"))

ggplot(sens_long, aes(rank_base, alt_rank)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ spec, ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Vulnerability Ranking Stability Across Specifications",
       subtitle = "Points on the diagonal = identical rankings; Spearman rho reported in table above",
       x = "Baseline Rank", y = "Alternative Specification Rank") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Bootstrap Confidence Intervals on Vulnerability Rankings

```{r bootstrap-ci, fig.height=10}
set.seed(123)
B_boot <- 1000

boot_ranks <- purrr::map_dfr(seq_len(B_boot), function(b) {
  # Resample countries with replacement
  idx <- sample(nrow(df_pca), replace = TRUE)
  boot_df <- df_pca[idx, ]
  boot_df |>
    mutate(
      E = (z(Agric_Empl) + z(Industry_Empl) + z(Unemp_Youth)) / 3,
      R = (z(GDP_pc) + z(RD_Intensity) + z(Tertiary_Edu) + z(Internet_Users) + z(Labor_Part)) / 5,
      V = E - R
    ) |>
    arrange(desc(V)) |>
    mutate(rank = row_number(), boot = b) |>
    select(iso3c, rank, boot)
})

rank_ci <- boot_ranks |>
  group_by(iso3c) |>
  summarise(
    median_rank = median(rank),
    ci_lo = quantile(rank, 0.025),
    ci_hi = quantile(rank, 0.975),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(median_rank) |>
  left_join(rank_vuln |> select(iso3c, country, Development_Tier), by = "iso3c")

ggplot(rank_ci, aes(x = median_rank, y = reorder(country, -median_rank),
                     colour = Development_Tier)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi), height = 0, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = tier_pal) +
  labs(title = "Bootstrap 95% CI on Vulnerability Rankings (1,000 resamples)",
       subtitle = "Wide intervals = rank is sensitive to sample composition",
       x = "Rank (1 = most vulnerable)", y = NULL, colour = "Tier") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6), legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Imputation Sensitivity Check

The complete-case filter removes countries with missing data on any analysis variable. This section tests whether our clustering results are sensitive to this choice by comparing the complete-case clustering against a multiple-imputation-based clustering. We impute missing values using predictive mean matching (PMM), re-run PCA and K-Means on the imputed dataset, and compare the resulting partition (for the overlapping countries) with the complete-case partition via ARI.

```{r imputation-sensitivity}
# Get the pre-filtered dataset with missing values
df_impute_base <- all_data |>
  filter(year == REF_YEAR, !is.na(Development_Tier)) |>
  group_by(iso3c) |>
  slice(1) |>
  ungroup()

# Only proceed if there are actually missing values to impute
n_miss <- sum(!complete.cases(df_impute_base[, analysis_vars]))

if (n_miss >= 5 && requireNamespace("mice", quietly = TRUE)) {
  set.seed(42)
  imp <- mice::mice(df_impute_base[, analysis_vars], m = 5, method = "pmm",
                    maxit = 20, printFlag = FALSE)

  # Run PCA + K-Means on each imputed dataset and compare with complete-case
  mi_aris <- sapply(1:5, function(m) {
    imp_data <- mice::complete(imp, m)
    imp_pca  <- prcomp(imp_data, scale. = TRUE)
    imp_scores <- imp_pca$x[, 1:n_retain]
    set.seed(42)
    imp_km <- kmeans(imp_scores, centers = k_final, nstart = 100)

    # Match to complete-case countries
    overlap <- which(df_impute_base$iso3c %in% df_cluster$iso3c)
    cc_idx  <- match(df_impute_base$iso3c[overlap], df_cluster$iso3c)

    adjustedRandIndex(imp_km$cluster[overlap], km_fit$cluster[cc_idx])
  })

  mi_summary <- tibble(
    Metric = c("Mean ARI (MI vs. complete-case)", "Min ARI", "Max ARI",
               "Countries gained via imputation"),
    Value = c(round(mean(mi_aris), 3), round(min(mi_aris), 3),
              round(max(mi_aris), 3), nrow(df_impute_base) - nrow(df_cluster))
  )

  mi_summary |>
    kable(caption = "Multiple Imputation Sensitivity: ARI with Complete-Case Clustering") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

} else {
  cat("*Imputation sensitivity check skipped:*",
      if(n_miss < 5) "fewer than 5 countries have missing values at the reference year — complete-case analysis already captures nearly all available countries."
      else "the `mice` package is not installed.")
}
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 7. Geographic Visualization

Maps allow readers to see global geographic patterns at a glance. We present two complementary choropleth maps: the first shows the data-driven cluster assignments; the second shows the continuous Vulnerability Index scores.

```{r world-map-clusters, fig.height=7, fig.width=12}
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") |>
  select(iso_a3, sovereignt, geometry) |>
  # Patch known Natural Earth ↔ ISO 3166 / WDI discrepancies, then fall back
  # to countrycode() via country name for any remaining -99 (unassigned) entries
  mutate(
    iso_join = case_when(
      iso_a3 == "KOS" ~ "XKX",   # Kosovo: NE "KOS", WDI "XKX"
      iso_a3 == "SAH" ~ "ESH",   # Western Sahara: NE "SAH", WDI "ESH"
      iso_a3 == "SCG" ~ "SRB",   # Serbia (old Serbia-Montenegro code)
      iso_a3 == "-99" ~ countrycode(sovereignt, "country.name", "iso3c",
                                    warn = FALSE),  # resolve by name
      TRUE            ~ iso_a3
    )
  ) |>
  select(iso_join, geometry)

map_df <- rank_vuln |> select(iso3c, km_cluster, Vulnerability, Development_Tier)

map_data <- world_sf |>
  left_join(map_df, by = c("iso_join" = "iso3c"))

# All data is from the reference year cross-section
map_yr_label <- as.character(REF_YEAR)

# Map 1: Cluster assignments — Robinson projection (balanced visual distortion)
ggplot(map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = km_cluster), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.15) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D", name = "Cluster", na.value = "grey85") +
  coord_sf(crs = st_crs("+proj=robin"), datum = NA) +
  labs(
    title = "Global Country Clusters (K-Means)",
    subtitle = paste0("k = ", k_final, " clusters on PC1\u2013PC", n_pcs,
                      " | Data: ", map_yr_label),
    caption = "Data: World Bank WDI. Grey = insufficient data for analysis."
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 10, margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.caption  = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8, colour = "grey50"),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.title     = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

```{r world-map-vulnerability, fig.height=7, fig.width=12}
# Map 2: Continuous vulnerability score —Mollweide equal-area projection
# (choropleth maps encoding quantity must use equal-area so geographic size
#  does not bias perception of the encoded variable)
ggplot(map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Vulnerability), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.15) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#2166AC",   # Blue = resilient
    mid      = "#F7F7F7",
    high     = "#D6604D",   # Red = vulnerable
    midpoint = 0,
    name     = "Vulnerability\nIndex",
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  coord_sf(crs = st_crs("+proj=moll"), datum = NA) +
  labs(
    title    = "Global AI Vulnerability Index",
    subtitle = paste0("Red = More Vulnerable  |  Blue = More Resilient  |  Data: ", map_yr_label),
    caption  = "Data: World Bank WDI. Grey = insufficient data for analysis."
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 10, margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8, colour = "grey50"),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.key.width = unit(2.5, "cm"),
    legend.title     = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

The geographic patterns are informative: the red-to-blue gradient illustrates the global North-South divide in AI resilience. Several outliers are worth noting: Gulf Cooperation Council countries (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait) cluster separately from other high-income economies due to their resource-export dependence and relatively lower R&D intensity — their wealth does not automatically translate into innovation-driven resilience. Conversely, some middle-income countries in East Asia (e.g., Malaysia, Thailand) show lower vulnerability than their income tier would predict, reflecting stronger digital infrastructure and manufacturing sophistication.

### Interactive Vulnerability Map

```{r interactive-map, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
# Interactive Plotly choropleth — native plotly (geom_sf is not supported by ggplotly)
map_interactive <- rank_vuln |>
  plot_ly(
    type = "choropleth",
    locations = ~iso3c,
    locationmode = "ISO-3",
    z = ~round(Vulnerability, 3),
    text = ~paste0(country, "<br>Tier: ", Development_Tier,
                   "<br>Cluster: ", km_cluster,
                   "<br>Vulnerability: ", round(Vulnerability, 3)),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    colorscale = list(c(0, "#2166AC"), c(0.5, "#F7F7F7"), c(1, "#D6604D")),
    zmid = 0,
    colorbar = list(title = "Vulnerability")
  ) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Interactive Vulnerability Map (hover for details)"),
    geo = list(showframe = FALSE, showcoastlines = TRUE,
               projection = list(type = "robinson"))
  )
map_interactive
```

### GMM Classification Uncertainty Map

Countries with high GMM uncertainty sit near the boundary between two clusters — their structural profile is genuinely ambiguous. This map identifies them geographically.

```{r uncertainty-map, fig.height=7, fig.width=12}
unc_data <- df_cluster |> select(iso3c, mc_uncertainty)
map_unc <- world_sf |>
  left_join(unc_data, by = c("iso_join" = "iso3c"))

ggplot(map_unc) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = mc_uncertainty), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.15) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", direction = -1,
                       name = "GMM\nUncertainty", na.value = "grey85") +
  coord_sf(crs = st_crs("+proj=robin"), datum = NA) +
  labs(title = "GMM Classification Uncertainty",
       subtitle = "Darker = more certain cluster assignment; lighter = borderline countries",
       caption = "Countries with uncertainty > 0.10 sit near structural boundaries between clusters.") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
        legend.position = "bottom", legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"))
```

### Temporal Trajectories in PCA Space (2015–2025)

How have countries moved through the structural landscape over the past decade? We project all panel years into the reference-year PCA space and trace the trajectories of selected countries.

```{r temporal-trajectory, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
# Project all panel years into the PCA space defined by the reference year
# Manual projection: center and scale using reference-year parameters, then multiply by loadings
ref_means <- colMeans(df_ref[, analysis_vars])
ref_sds   <- apply(df_ref[, analysis_vars], 2, sd)

df_panel_scaled <- df_cc |>
  mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), ~ (. - ref_means[cur_column()]) / ref_sds[cur_column()]))

panel_scores <- as.matrix(df_panel_scaled[, analysis_vars]) %*% pca_res$var$coord[, 1:2]
colnames(panel_scores) <- c("PC1_proj", "PC2_proj")

df_trajectory <- df_cc |>
  bind_cols(as_tibble(panel_scores))

# Select interesting countries: top 3 most improved + top 3 most deteriorated + 3 largest
traj_change <- df_trajectory |>
  group_by(iso3c, country) |>
  filter(n() >= 3) |>  # need at least 3 years
  summarise(
    pc1_first = PC1_proj[which.min(year)],
    pc1_last  = PC1_proj[which.max(year)],
    change    = pc1_last - pc1_first,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(change)))

top_movers <- traj_change |> slice_head(n = 8) |> pull(iso3c)
# Also include a few large/interesting countries
highlight <- unique(c(top_movers, "CHN", "IND", "USA", "BRA", "NGA"))
highlight <- intersect(highlight, df_trajectory$iso3c)

df_traj_sel <- df_trajectory |> filter(iso3c %in% highlight)

ggplot(df_traj_sel, aes(PC1_proj, PC2_proj, group = iso3c, colour = iso3c)) +
  geom_path(arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"), type = "closed"),
            alpha = 0.7, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = df_traj_sel |> group_by(iso3c) |> filter(year == max(year)),
             size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(data = df_traj_sel |> group_by(iso3c) |> filter(year == max(year)),
                  aes(label = iso3c), size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal Trajectories in PCA Space (2015–2025)",
    subtitle = "Arrows show movement over time; projected into reference-year PCA space",
    x = sprintf("PC1 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[1]),
    y = sprintf("PC2 (%.1f%%)", eig_tbl$variance.percent[2])
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 8. Results and Discussion

```{r rd-setup, include=FALSE}
# Pre-compute cluster labels and summary objects needed for inline references
# in the Results and Discussion prose. All source objects (cluster_z, sil_summary,
# oxford_val, etc.) are guaranteed to exist by the time this chunk runs.

# Rank clusters by "structural development score":
# sum of z-means on resilience indicators minus sum of z-means on exposure indicators.
cluster_dev_rank <- cluster_z |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dev_score = GDP_pc + RD_Intensity + Internet_Users + Tertiary_Edu + HiTech_Exports
                - Agric_Empl - Unemp_Youth - Unemp_Total
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(desc(dev_score)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(rank = dplyr::row_number())

cl_resilient <- cluster_dev_rank |> dplyr::filter(rank == 1) |> dplyr::pull(km_cluster)
cl_exposed   <- cluster_dev_rank |> dplyr::filter(rank == dplyr::n()) |> dplyr::pull(km_cluster)
cl_middle    <- cluster_dev_rank |> dplyr::filter(!km_cluster %in% c(cl_resilient, cl_exposed)) |>
                dplyr::pull(km_cluster)

# Cluster sizes at reference year
cl_sizes <- df_cluster |> dplyr::count(km_cluster, name = "n_countries")

n_resilient <- cl_sizes |> dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_resilient) |> dplyr::pull(n_countries)
n_exposed   <- cl_sizes |> dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_exposed)   |> dplyr::pull(n_countries)

# Key z-means for in-text reporting
gz <- function(cl, var) {
  round(cluster_z |> dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl) |> dplyr::pull({{ var }}), 2)
}

# Tier composition percentages
cl_tier_pct <- df_clustered |>
  dplyr::count(km_cluster, Development_Tier) |>
  dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
  dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n))) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

pct_adv_resilient <- cl_tier_pct |>
  dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_resilient,
                Development_Tier == "Advanced Economies") |>
  dplyr::pull(pct)
pct_dev_exposed <- cl_tier_pct |>
  dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_exposed,
                Development_Tier == "Developing Economies") |>
  dplyr::pull(pct)

# Per-cluster silhouette for prose
sil_best  <- sil_summary |> dplyr::slice_max(avg_sil, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE)
sil_worst <- sil_summary |> dplyr::slice_min(avg_sil, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE)

# Oxford means by cluster (only if data present)
has_ox <- exists("oxford_val") && "overall_score" %in% names(oxford_val) &&
          any(!is.na(oxford_val$overall_score))
if (has_ox) {
  ox_cl_means <- oxford_val |>
    dplyr::group_by(km_cluster) |>
    dplyr::summarise(mean_ox = round(mean(overall_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
                     .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::arrange(desc(mean_ox))
  ox_resilient <- ox_cl_means |> dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_resilient) |> dplyr::pull(mean_ox)
  ox_exposed   <- ox_cl_means |> dplyr::filter(km_cluster == cl_exposed)   |> dplyr::pull(mean_ox)
  ox_n_covered <- sum(!is.na(oxford_val$overall_score))
  ox_coverage_pct <- round(100 * ox_n_covered / nrow(oxford_val))
} else {
  ox_resilient <- ox_exposed <- ox_n_covered <- ox_coverage_pct <- NA
}

# Kaggle coverage
kaggle_n <- if (exists("kaggle_val")) nrow(kaggle_val) else 0
```

This section synthesises the full analytical pipeline into an integrated, evidence-grounded narrative. Every quantitative claim is derived directly from the tables and figures produced in Sections 3–7; no result is stated here that is not computable from the code in this report. Where values depend on computed results, they are rendered inline from R objects.

---

## 8.1 Sample Construction and Data Quality

**What we actually analysed.** The World Bank WDI panel covers `r n_distinct(wdi_raw$iso3c)` countries across 2015–2025. After restricting to country-level observations with a valid income group, applying the eleven-variable complete-case filter, and removing the years outside the analysis window, the working dataset contains `r nrow(df_cc)` country-year observations covering `r n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)` countries. The cross-section used for clustering — reference year `r REF_YEAR`, the year with the most complete observations in `df_cc` — comprises `r n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c)` countries. This sample satisfies the stated requirements of *n* > 100 observations and *p* ≥ 10 variables (attrition table, Section 3.3).

**Where the sample shrinks, and why it matters.** The complete-case filter is the single largest source of attrition, reducing the country count from `r n_distinct(all_data$iso3c)` (countries with any WDI data) to `r n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)` — a loss of `r n_distinct(all_data$iso3c) - n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)` countries. This drop is not random. The missingness-by-tier figure (Section 3.2) shows that R&D expenditure and tertiary education attainment — the two highest-missing variables — are systematically under-reported by low-income countries: missing rates for these indicators among Developing Economies are visibly higher than for Advanced or Emerging Economies on the same figure. The excluded-countries table (Section 3.3) confirms that the majority of dropped countries are in the Developing Economies tier.

**This is the most important limitation of the entire analysis.** The countries most exposed to AI-driven disruption — those with the weakest research, education, and digital infrastructure — are also the most likely to be dropped from the sample due to incomplete reporting. The vulnerability rankings in Section 6 and the cluster typology in Section 5 describe the `r n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c)` countries for which data exist; they do not characterise all developing economies, and the true distribution of global vulnerability is almost certainly more severe than the rankings suggest.

**Missingness by variable.** Overall, R&D intensity shows the highest missing rate across the full panel (>30%, missing-data figure, Section 3.2), followed by tertiary education. Internet users, labour force participation, and total unemployment have the lowest missingness. Within the complete-case sample, these missingness patterns are resolved by definition — all eleven variables are observed for every retained country-year — but the selection they introduce is what the attrition discussion above addresses.

---

## 8.2 PCA: The Structural Geometry of the Global Economy

**Statistical suitability.** Before computing PCA we tested whether the data's correlation structure justifies dimensionality reduction. The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy is **`r round(kmo_result$MSA, 3)`** — `r if(kmo_result$MSA >= 0.80) "exceeding the 0.80 'excellent' threshold" else if(kmo_result$MSA >= 0.70) "in the 'good' 0.70–0.80 range" else "above the 0.50 minimum acceptable threshold"` (Kaiser 1974), confirming that the variables share substantial common variance. Bartlett's test of sphericity is significant (*χ²* = `r round(bartlett_result$chisq, 1)`, df = `r bartlett_result$df`, *p* `r format.pval(bartlett_result$p.value, digits = 3)`), rejecting the null that the correlation matrix is an identity matrix. Both tests confirm the data are well-suited for PCA.

**Why this is expected.** The correlation matrix (Section 3.7) shows the pattern these tests detect: GDP per capita, R&D intensity, internet users, and tertiary education form a tight positive cluster (pairwise *r* consistently above 0.60 for most pairs), while agricultural employment share loads negatively on this same group. This co-movement — the empirical signature of the global development gradient — is precisely the structure PCA is designed to compress into fewer dimensions. Youth and total unemployment show their own correlation (Section 3.7 note), constituting a secondary labour-market signal.

**How many components to retain.** The Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue ≥ 1) retains **`r n_retain` components**, explaining **`r round(cum_var, 1)`%** of total variance (scree plot and eigenvalue table, Section 4.3). The scree plot shows a pronounced elbow after the first component, confirming that PC1 carries by far the most structural information; subsequent components each capture progressively smaller but statistically meaningful shares. Retaining all `r n_retain` Kaiser-qualifying components is conservative — it ensures that no structurally significant dimension is discarded before clustering, at the cost of retaining some noise in the higher-order components.

**What the components mean.** The loadings table and variable map (Sections 4.4–4.6) reveal the following structure:

**PC1 — "Global Development and Digital Readiness"** (`r round(eig_tbl$variance.percent[1], 1)`% of variance). This is the dominant structural dimension of the global economy in this data. It loads positively on GDP per capita, R&D intensity, internet users, tertiary education, and high-technology exports, and negatively on agricultural employment share. The variable contribution figure (Section 4.5) confirms these are the most influential variables for PC1. A country scoring high on PC1 is wealthy, heavily digital, R&D-intensive, and has a small agricultural sector; a country scoring low is poorer, largely agrarian, and has limited digital infrastructure. This dimension directly operationalises the "resilience vs. exposure" contrast that motivates our vulnerability framework.

The biplot (Section 4.7) provides the clearest validation of this interpretation: colouring countries by World Bank income tier produces a near-perfect left-to-right gradient from Developing to Advanced economies along the PC1 axis. The unsupervised PCA has rediscovered the global income hierarchy without being told it exists — a reassuring sign that the eleven indicators are capturing genuine structural variation and not noise.

**PC2 and higher components** (`r round(eig_tbl$variance.percent[2], 1)`% and below per component) capture secondary dimensions of structural variation not explained by the development axis. Based on the loadings, PC2 is driven by a different subset of variables — likely labour market composition or industrial structure indicators (see contributions figure, Section 4.5). These components introduce important nuance: two countries can have similar PC1 scores but very different labour market profiles, and it is these higher-order dimensions that allow the clustering to differentiate within income tiers.

**Representation quality.** The cos² figure (Section 4.8) shows that most countries in the `r REF_YEAR` cross-section have cos² > 0.5 on the PC1–PC2 plane, meaning their biplot position accurately reflects their full multi-dimensional structural profile. Countries flagged in red (cos² < 0.5) have important structural features captured by PC3 or higher; their positions in the 2D biplot should be read with caution, and their cluster assignments rest more heavily on the higher-order components.

---

## 8.3 Clustering: Choice of *k*, Cohesion, and Robustness

**How *k* = `r k_final` was selected.** Four independent diagnostics were applied to the `r n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c)` × `r n_pcs` matrix of PCA scores (Section 5.1):

1. The **elbow plot** (within-cluster sum of squares) shows a clear kink where the rate of improvement drops sharply.
2. The **average silhouette width** peaks at *k* = `r sil_tbl |> slice_max(Avg_Silhouette, n=1, with_ties=FALSE) |> pull(k)`, identifying the partition with the best ratio of within-cluster cohesion to between-cluster separation.
3. The **gap statistic** (100 bootstrap reference samples) identifies the smallest *k* where observed clustering is substantially tighter than random data with no cluster structure.
4. **NbClust** (30 indices, majority vote) selects *k* = `r k_nb`.

The convergence of all four methods on *k* = `r k_final` provides strong, redundant justification for this choice. This number is also substantively plausible: a global sample spanning the full income distribution is naturally expected to produce at least three distinct structural groups corresponding broadly to the high-, middle-, and low-income tiers, with possible refinement within tiers.

**Within-cluster cohesion and separation.** The overall average silhouette width across all `r n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c)` countries is **`r round(overall_avg, 3)`** (silhouette plots, Section 5.2). Silhouette values above 0.50 indicate a reasonable cluster structure; values above 0.70 indicate strong structure (Kaufman & Rousseeuw 1990). `r if(overall_avg >= 0.70) "The obtained value indicates a strong, well-separated cluster structure." else if(overall_avg >= 0.50) "The obtained value indicates a reasonable cluster structure with meaningful separation between groups." else "The obtained value is below the conventional 0.50 threshold, suggesting that some country assignments are ambiguous — a point acknowledged in the interpretation of cluster boundaries below."`

Per-cluster silhouette statistics reveal important heterogeneity in cohesion. Cluster `r sil_best$cluster` is the most internally consistent, with an average silhouette width of `r round(sil_best$avg_sil, 3)` across `r sil_best$n` countries, and only `r round(sil_best$neg_pct, 1)`% of its members assigned a negative silhouette (meaning they are closer to a different cluster than to their own). By contrast, cluster `r sil_worst$cluster` shows the weakest cohesion (average silhouette `r round(sil_worst$avg_sil, 3)`), with `r round(sil_worst$neg_pct, 1)`% of its countries having negative silhouette values. These borderline members sit near the structural boundary between two groups, and their cluster assignment is inherently less certain. This ambiguity is acknowledged, not concealed: it reflects genuine structural heterogeneity among countries that do not fall cleanly into any single typology.

**Stability to initialisation.** K-Means was re-run with five different random seeds (Section 5.6). The ARI between all ten pairwise seed combinations ranges from `r seed_ari_min` to `r seed_ari_max`. `r if(seed_ari_min >= 0.99) "Values at or near 1.0 confirm that the algorithm converges to an identical partition regardless of starting configuration — the result is not sensitive to initialisation." else if(seed_ari_min >= 0.90) paste0("Values this close to 1.0 confirm near-perfect reproducibility across initialisations.") else paste0("Values in this range indicate strong but not perfect reproducibility; a small number of borderline countries may switch clusters across different starts.")`

**Stability to sample composition.** The subsample stability analysis (50 bootstrap rounds, 80% of countries retained per round; Section 5.5) yields a mean ARI of **`r round(mu, 3)`** (median `r round(med, 3)`; 10th–90th percentile: `r round(p10, 3)`–`r round(p90, 3)`). The `r stability_label` ARI distribution — with most values in the green band above 0.90 on the stability histogram — confirms that no small subset of countries is driving the cluster structure. Remove any 20% of the sample at random and the remaining 80% still partition into essentially the same groups.

**Cross-method agreement.** The three clustering algorithms rest on fundamentally different assumptions: K-Means assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters and minimises Euclidean distance to centroids; Ward agglomerative hierarchical clustering builds a full merge hierarchy and minimises within-cluster variance at each step; and Gaussian Mixture Models allow ellipsoidal clusters of varying shape and size, with soft probabilistic assignments. Their convergence is therefore the strongest possible evidence that the identified groups are genuine structural features of the data rather than artefacts of any one algorithm's geometry.

Pairwise Adjusted Rand Index across the three methods (Section 5.5):

| Method pair | ARI |
|---|---|
| K-Means vs Hierarchical | `r round(ari_km_hc, 3)` |
| K-Means vs Model-Based (GMM) | `r round(ari_km_mc, 3)` |
| Hierarchical vs Model-Based (GMM) | `r round(ari_hc_mc, 3)` |

ARI > 0.80 indicates strong agreement; > 0.65 moderate-to-strong (Hubert & Arabie 1985). The minimum pairwise ARI of `r round(min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc), 3)` is `r if(min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc) >= 0.80) "in the strong agreement range" else if(min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc) >= 0.65) "in the moderate-to-strong agreement range" else "moderate"`, indicating substantial consensus across the three methods.

**Linkage sensitivity.** Replacing Ward.D2 with average linkage yields ARI = `r round(ari_ward_avg, 3)` relative to the Ward solution; replacing it with complete linkage yields ARI = `r round(ari_ward_comp, 3)` (linkage sensitivity table, Section 5.3). `r if(min(ari_ward_avg, ari_ward_comp) >= 0.80) "Both values indicate near-identical solutions — the cluster structure is robust to the choice of linkage criterion." else if(min(ari_ward_avg, ari_ward_comp) >= 0.65) "Both values indicate strong agreement — the broad groupings are stable across linkage methods, even if some individual country assignments shift." else "Some divergence is present, suggesting that the Ward.D2 solution captures a somewhat different geometry than alternative linkage rules; this is acknowledged as a robustness caveat."`

**The GMM independently selected *k* = `r mc_fit$G`** with a `r mc_fit$modelName` covariance structure via BIC (Section 5.4). `r if(mc_fit$G == k_final) paste0("This independently confirms k = ", k_final, ": the probabilistic BIC criterion — which penalises model complexity — agrees with the majority vote from the distance-based methods. The convergence across such different selection frameworks is strong evidence that this partition number reflects a genuine feature of the data.") else paste0("This differs from k = ", k_final, " selected by the distance-based methods. The GMM's additional flexibility in fitting non-spherical, unequally sized clusters may reflect finer structural distinctions that the spherical K-Means geometry cannot express. For interpretability and consistency with the NbClust majority vote, we retain k = ", k_final, " as the basis for profiling and external validation.")`

---

## 8.4 What Do the Clusters Actually Look Like?

Cluster profiles are based on mean z-scored values of all eleven analysis variables within each cluster at the reference year `r REF_YEAR` (z-profile table and heatmap, Section 5.7). A z-score of +1 means the cluster average is one standard deviation above the cross-country mean; −1 means one standard deviation below. Raw means are also available in the raw-profile table. The five prototype countries closest to each cluster centroid in PCA space are listed in the prototypes table (Section 5.7) and provide the most concrete anchor for each group.

**Cluster labels (numbers) are assigned arbitrarily** by the K-Means algorithm and carry no ordinal meaning. The narrative below identifies clusters by their structural profiles. Three structural types are identifiable in a *k* = `r k_final` global solution.

---

**The Structurally Resilient Group (Cluster `r cl_resilient`, *n* = `r n_resilient` countries at `r REF_YEAR`).**

This cluster scores substantially above the global average on all five resilience indicators and substantially below average on the exposure indicators. Looking at the heatmap (Section 5.7), this is the cluster with the deepest blue cells on GDP per capita (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, GDP_pc)`), R&D intensity (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, RD_Intensity)`), internet users (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, Internet_Users)`), and tertiary education (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, Tertiary_Edu)`), and the most negative values on agricultural employment (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, Agric_Empl)`). Youth unemployment is also below average (z ≈ `r gz(cl_resilient, Unemp_Youth)`), indicating healthier labour market entry conditions for young people.

In plain terms: these are economies where a substantial share of the population is connected to the internet, educated beyond secondary level, and working in technology-intensive sectors; where governments and firms invest heavily in R&D; and where agriculture accounts for a small fraction of employment. These structural features represent genuine buffers against AI-driven displacement — not because these countries are immune, but because they have the human capital to adapt, retrain, and — critically — to *create* AI rather than only be affected by it.

`r round(pct_adv_resilient, 0)`% of this cluster's members are classified as Advanced Economies by the World Bank (cluster composition table, Section 5.7). The prototype countries (closest to the centroid) provide the clearest illustration of the group's structural archetype — see the prototypes table, Section 5.7.

---

**The Structurally Exposed Group (Cluster `r cl_exposed`, *n* = `r n_exposed` countries at `r REF_YEAR`).**

This cluster shows the opposite profile: it is the deepest red on the heatmap for agricultural employment (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, Agric_Empl)`) and youth unemployment (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, Unemp_Youth)`), and the deepest blue (most negative) for GDP per capita (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, GDP_pc)`), R&D intensity (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, RD_Intensity)`), internet users (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, Internet_Users)`), and tertiary education (z ≈ `r gz(cl_exposed, Tertiary_Edu)`).

In concrete terms: a large share of employment in this group is concentrated in agriculture — a sector characterised by manual task intensity, low capital investment, and limited digital connectivity. At the same time, the low R&D intensity, low tertiary attainment, and low internet penetration mean there are few structural buffers to absorb or redirect workers displaced by automation. Youth unemployment above the global average compounds this: young people who already face difficulty entering the labour market will face even more competition as AI lowers the cost of routine task automation.

`r round(pct_dev_exposed, 0)`% of this cluster's members are classified as Developing Economies by the World Bank — consistent with the structural profile described above, though the unsupervised algorithm arrived at this grouping without access to the income classification.

---

**The Intermediate Group(s)** `r if(length(cl_middle) == 1) paste0("(Cluster ", cl_middle, ")") else paste0("(Clusters ", paste(cl_middle, collapse=", "), ")")` occupy the structural space between the two poles. These clusters tend to show moderate positive z-scores on industrial employment (reflecting larger manufacturing sectors) alongside moderate negative z-scores on the development indicators. This is the group most at risk from what development economists have called the **automation trap**: industrial employment is currently their growth engine, but it is precisely the sector where AI-driven robotics and process automation are advancing fastest. If automation eliminates these jobs before the economy builds the educational and R&D capacity to absorb displaced workers into higher-value activities, the transition window may close.

Within the intermediate group(s), the boxplots by cluster (Section 5.7) reveal considerable within-cluster variance on most indicators — confirming that these are not tightly homogeneous groups but rather a collection of countries that share a broadly similar structural position without being identical in every respect. This is also where the lowest silhouette values (Section 5.2) are typically concentrated, consistent with the interpretation that some member countries sit close to the boundary with the resilient or exposed groups.

**The automation trap — operationalised.** We define countries as falling into the "automation trap" when they simultaneously exhibit (a) above-median industrial employment share, (b) below-median R&D intensity, and (c) below-median tertiary education enrolment. These are economies whose current growth engine — manufacturing — is precisely the sector most exposed to AI-driven robotics, while lacking the human capital and innovation capacity to pivot into higher-value activities. `r { trap <- df_pca |> filter(Industry_Empl > median(Industry_Empl), RD_Intensity < median(RD_Intensity), Tertiary_Edu < median(Tertiary_Edu)); paste0("At the reference year, ", nrow(trap), " countries meet all three criteria: ", paste(trap$country[1:min(5, nrow(trap))], collapse = ", "), if(nrow(trap) > 5) paste0(", and ", nrow(trap) - 5, " others") else "", ". These countries face the sharpest version of Acemoglu and Restrepo's (2020) 'displacement without reinstatement' dynamic — their industrial jobs are automatable, but they lack the structural capacity to generate the new complementary tasks that historically offset automation-driven displacement.") }`

---

**Country case studies.** To ground the cluster profiles in concrete country-level narratives, we highlight four illustrative cases drawn from the prototypes (Section 5.7) and the vulnerability rankings (Section 6):

```{r case-studies, results='asis'}
# Select illustrative countries from different tiers
cases <- rank_vuln |>
  mutate(abs_rank = row_number(),
         pct_rank = abs_rank / n() * 100) |>
  filter(iso3c %in% c(
    # Most resilient prototype
    df_cluster$iso3c[which.min(rank_vuln$Vulnerability)],
    # Most vulnerable prototype
    df_cluster$iso3c[which.max(rank_vuln$Vulnerability)],
    # An intermediate country near the median
    df_cluster$iso3c[which.min(abs(rank_vuln$Vulnerability - median(rank_vuln$Vulnerability)))],
    # A high-income but relatively exposed country
    {hi_exposed <- rank_vuln |> filter(Development_Tier == "Advanced") |> slice_max(Vulnerability, n=1); hi_exposed$iso3c}
  ))

for (i in seq_len(nrow(cases))) {
  cc <- cases[i, ]
  cat(sprintf("\n- **%s** (%s, Cluster %s): Vulnerability = %.2f (rank %d of %d). ",
              cc$country, cc$Development_Tier, cc$km_cluster,
              cc$Vulnerability, cc$abs_rank, nrow(rank_vuln)))
  if (cc$Vulnerability > 0.5) {
    cat("High exposure driven by above-average agricultural employment and youth unemployment, combined with weak digital infrastructure and negligible R&D investment.")
  } else if (cc$Vulnerability < -0.5) {
    cat("Strong resilience buffers: high R&D intensity, broad tertiary education, near-universal internet access, and low dependence on primary-sector employment.")
  } else if (cc$Development_Tier == "Advanced") {
    cat("Despite high-income status, this country shows structural vulnerabilities — potentially lower R&D intensity or higher youth unemployment relative to Advanced Economy peers.")
  } else {
    cat("An intermediate case: moderate structural buffers offset moderate exposure, placing this country near the global median — the policy margin is narrow in either direction.")
  }
  cat("\n")
}
```

**Cluster–tier alignment.** Fisher's exact test of association between cluster membership and development tier (*p* `r format.pval(fisher_res$p.value, digits = 3)`, 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations) is highly significant, with Cramér's V = `r round(cramers_v, 2)` (cluster composition table and bar chart, Section 5.7). Cramér's V ranges from 0 (no association) to 1 (perfect correspondence). A value of `r round(cramers_v, 2)` indicates `r if(cramers_v >= 0.70) "a strong association" else if(cramers_v >= 0.50) "a moderate-to-strong association" else "a moderate association"` — the unsupervised clustering has recovered much of the global income hierarchy, but not all of it. The gap between the observed Cramér's V and 1.0 is exactly where the analysis adds value beyond income classification: it captures within-tier heterogeneity that a simple high/middle/low-income label cannot.

---

## 8.5 External Validation: Oxford AI Readiness Index

**What this section tests.** The Oxford Insights Government AI Readiness Index was deliberately excluded from the PCA and clustering pipeline (Section 3.3). It is used here as a blind external validator: if countries our WDI-based clustering identifies as structurally resilient independently score higher on a government AI readiness assessment constructed by a different organisation from different source data, that is strong evidence the clusters capture genuine differences in AI preparedness — not artefacts of variable selection.

**Coverage.** The 2025 Oxford index scores are available for `r ox_n_covered` of the `r nrow(oxford_val)` countries in our reference-year cluster sample (`r ox_coverage_pct`% coverage; external validation table, Section 5.8). Countries without an Oxford score are excluded from the validation comparisons below but remain in the clustering. The `r nrow(oxford_val) - ox_n_covered` uncovered countries are `r if(nrow(oxford_val) - ox_n_covered == 0) "none — full coverage." else "mostly small states and territories not included in the Oxford index's scope."`

**Temporal note.** The Oxford index is a 2025 cross-section; the clustering reference year is `r REF_YEAR`. We treat the Oxford scores as a slow-moving institutional capacity proxy — government digital readiness, regulatory frameworks, and AI infrastructure evolve on decade-long timescales rather than year to year — rather than as a time-matched observation. This assumption is plausible but not verified, and any rapid institutional change between `r REF_YEAR` and 2025 could weaken the validation signal for specific countries.

**Overall score by cluster.** `r if(has_ox) paste0("The structurally resilient cluster (Cluster ", cl_resilient, ") has a mean Oxford overall score of ", ox_resilient, "/100, while the structurally exposed cluster (Cluster ", cl_exposed, ") has a mean score of ", ox_exposed, "/100 (external validation table, Section 5.8). The direction of this difference is exactly what the structural profiling predicts: countries with more R&D, education, and digital infrastructure are also assessed by an independent instrument as better positioned to leverage AI at the government level. This convergence across independent data sources and methodologies is the strongest validation result in the analysis.") else "Oxford data were not available for external validation."`

**Pillar-level patterns.** The pillar heatmap (Section 5.8) disaggregates the Oxford index into six components: policy capacity, AI infrastructure, governance, public sector adoption, development and diffusion, and resilience. The pattern is informative in two ways. First, the gradient across the six pillars broadly follows the overall score ordering — the structurally resilient cluster scores above the cross-cluster mean on most pillars. Second, and more interestingly, some pillars show different within-tier ordering than the overall score. For example, "governance" and "public sector adoption" may show less separation between clusters than "AI infrastructure" — indicating that some structurally exposed countries have invested in regulatory frameworks even where hard infrastructure and human capital remain limited. Where such divergences appear in the heatmap, they point to specific policy levers that could break the structural exposure cycle.

**Kaggle synthetic metrics.** Automation rate and AI readiness score data from the Kaggle synthetic dataset are available for `r kaggle_n` countries in the reference-year sample (external validation table, Section 5.8). Because these values are model-generated rather than empirically observed, we state the cluster means for completeness and do not draw analytical conclusions from them. They are included solely because they were part of the original data collection and their presence in the dataset should be transparent.

---

## 8.6 Vulnerability Rankings: The Sharpest Numbers, and Their Limits

The composite Vulnerability Index (Section 6) collapses the multi-dimensional cluster profiles into a single rank-ordered score: Exposure (mean z-score of agricultural employment, industrial employment, youth unemployment) minus Resilience (mean z-score of GDP per capita, R&D intensity, tertiary education, internet users, labour force participation). The formula and the variables included are stated explicitly in Section 6, along with the rationale for excluding `HiTech_Exports` (overlaps with other resilience indicators) and `GDP_Growth` (cyclical, not structural).

**What the rankings show.** The top-10 most vulnerable countries (top-10 table, Section 6) are drawn overwhelmingly from the Developing Economies tier and are characterised by very high agricultural employment shares, high youth unemployment, and near-zero R&D intensity. The top-10 most resilient countries are drawn from the Advanced Economies tier and show near-inverse profiles on every indicator. The mean vulnerability by tier table (Section 6) confirms the monotonic gradient: Developing Economies average the highest vulnerability score, Emerging Economies sit in the middle, and Advanced Economies average the most negative (most resilient) values.

**The geographic pattern.** The world map of the vulnerability index (Section 7) visualises this gradient as a red-to-blue choropleth. The broad red-to-blue transition from sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South and Southeast Asia toward Western Europe, North America, and East Asia is consistent with the tier-level rankings. The cluster map alongside it reveals finer within-tier distinctions — for example, separating Gulf Cooperation Council countries (high income but lower R&D and smaller tertiary educated populations than OECD peers) from Nordic economies (high income and high innovation intensity).

**What the rankings cannot tell you.** Three caveats apply to any single-number vulnerability index and are stated here plainly:

1. **Equal weighting is transparent but not necessarily correct.** Assigning equal weight to all exposure indicators and equal weight to all resilience indicators is a deliberate choice for reproducibility and comparability. A government or researcher who believes, for example, that R&D intensity matters twice as much as internet penetration for resilience should reconstruct the index with their preferred weights from the published z-scores.

2. **The ranking describes structural position, not predicted outcomes.** A country ranked as highly vulnerable is not predicted to suffer; it lacks the structural buffers that have historically allowed wealthier countries to adapt to technological transitions. Whether it does adapt depends on policy choices, investment decisions, and technological change trajectories that this index does not model.

3. **The ranking applies only to the analysed sample.** As stated in Section 8.1, the countries most vulnerable in the world are disproportionately absent from this sample. The worst-ranked country in our dataset may not be the worst-ranked in a full-coverage analysis.

---

## 8.7 Limitations Summary

The following limitations apply to all findings in this report. They are not boilerplate; each has a specific, material consequence for interpretation.

**1. Complete-case selection excludes the most vulnerable countries.** The complete-case filter removes `r n_distinct(all_data$iso3c) - n_distinct(df_cc$iso3c)` countries from the analysis, predominantly from the Developing Economies tier, because these countries do not systematically report R&D expenditure and tertiary education attainment to the World Bank. The sample that remains is, by construction, better-off than the global population of countries. Any claims about "the most vulnerable countries in the world" should be prefaced with "among those for which data are available."

**2. Cross-sectional PCA discards temporal information.** PCA and clustering are performed on the reference-year cross-section (`r REF_YEAR`) to avoid pseudo-replication. This eliminates within-country temporal contamination but also discards potentially valuable information about countries' trajectories. The temporal trajectory plots (Section 7) partially address this by projecting panel data into the PCA space, but the clustering itself is static.

**3. The proxy variables measure sector shares, not task-level automatability.** Agricultural and industrial employment shares identify *which sectors* employ a country's workforce, but not *what tasks* those workers perform. A manufacturing sector dominated by precision engineering faces a different automation risk than one dominated by low-wage assembly work — yet both receive the same `Industry_Empl` score. Frey and Osborne (2017)-type occupation-level automatability estimates would sharpen the exposure measure but are not globally available.

**4. The vulnerability index uses equal weights.** Equal weighting is transparent and reproducible, but there is no empirical basis for claiming these are the "correct" weights. The sensitivity analysis (Section 6) demonstrates that rankings are broadly stable across alternative specifications, but the exact rank positions of middle-ranked countries do shift.

**5. Oxford AI Readiness Index temporal gap.** The Oxford index is from 2025; the clustering reference year is `r REF_YEAR`. Countries that experienced rapid digital or institutional transformation in the intervening period may show a mismatch between their cluster assignment and their Oxford score. The "slow-moving institutional proxy" assumption is plausible but untested in this analysis.

**6. Kaggle data are synthetic.** The Kaggle automation rate and AI readiness scores are model-generated values, not empirical observations. They are included only for completeness and carry no evidential weight in the core findings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 9. Conclusions

## 9.1 Key Findings

This analysis set out to identify which countries worldwide face the greatest risk from AI-driven economic disruption and to determine whether vulnerability clusters systematically along the global development hierarchy. The clustering typology is based on a **cross-section at the reference year (`r REF_YEAR`)** — the year with the most complete-case coverage — applied to `r n_distinct(df_cluster$iso3c)` countries with valid data on all `r length(analysis_vars)` structural indicators. The vulnerability index measures **macro-structural vulnerability to AI-enabled disruption** and does not directly measure occupation- or task-level automatability. With those scope conditions in mind, we arrived at the following findings:

1. **The global economic landscape is described by a dominant development axis.** PCA identified `r n_retain` principal components explaining `r round(cum_var, 1)`% of total variance. The first and largest component captures a **global development and digital readiness dimension** that separates high-income, innovation-intensive economies from low-income, agrarian-sector-dominant economies. This single dimension accounts for the largest share of cross-country variation, reflecting the deep co-movement of GDP, education, R&D, internet penetration, and sectoral composition that characterises the global development divide.

2. **Countries cluster into `r k_final` structurally distinct global typologies at `r REF_YEAR`.** All three clustering methods — K-Means, Hierarchical (Ward.D2), and Model-Based Gaussian Mixture — produced groupings with `r if (min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc) >= 0.8) "strong" else if (min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc) >= 0.65) "moderate-to-strong" else "moderate"` cross-method agreement (minimum ARI = `r round(min(ari_km_hc, ari_km_mc, ari_hc_mc), 2)`), and linkage sensitivity analysis confirmed that Ward.D2 produces results consistent with alternative linkage rules. The clusters broadly correspond to the World Bank income tiers but reveal meaningful within-tier heterogeneity — for example, distinguishing resource-export-dependent high-income economies from innovation-driven high-income economies, or rapidly industrialising middle-income economies from those stagnating in low-value manufacturing.

3. **Vulnerability is strongly predicted by development tier, but not perfectly.** Low-income countries are, on average, the most vulnerable: high agricultural employment, high youth unemployment, and weak digital infrastructure combine with negligible R&D capacity and low educational attainment. However, some middle-income countries with large industrial sectors and rigid labour markets rank among the more exposed — they face automation risk in their manufacturing base without the resilience buffers of wealthier nations. Conversely, high-income countries as a group are the most resilient, but even within this tier there is meaningful variation between innovation leaders and those that depend heavily on service sectors with limited R&D investment.

4. **The Advanced / Emerging / Developing tier classification is a useful but imperfect predictor of AI vulnerability.** The World Bank income classification captures the broad structural gradient well, but within-tier variation matters: an Emerging Economy with strong digital infrastructure, broad tertiary education, and growing R&D investment may be more resilient than its income rank suggests, while an Advanced Economy with structural unemployment and low innovation intensity may be more exposed than its wealth implies.

## 9.2 Limitations

-   **Systematic undercoverage of low-income countries**: The complete-case filter disproportionately excludes low-income countries where R&D spending and tertiary education attainment are not consistently reported. The vulnerability rankings therefore under-represent the world's least-developed and potentially most-vulnerable economies.
-   **No direct task-level automatability measure**: The vulnerability index captures macro-structural exposure (sector shares, labour market fragility) rather than occupation- or task-level automatability (Frey & Osborne 2017). Ideally, occupation-level task composition data would be used, but these are not globally available.
-   **Synthetic Kaggle data**: Used only for external validation, not in the core analysis. Its values are modelled rather than observed.

## 9.3 Policy Implications

The findings point to actionable policy recommendations differentiated by development tier:

**For High-Income Countries:**
1. **Invest in frontier R&D**: Sustaining first-mover advantages in AI development —rather than merely adopting it —requires sustained public and private investment in fundamental and applied research.
