2. **Proactive labour market policies**: Even resilient economies face structural displacement in specific occupations. Anticipatory reskilling programmes, portable social protection, and active labour market policies should be institutionalised before disruption peaks.

**For Middle-Income Countries:**
3. **Avoid the automation trap**: Countries in the middle of industrialisation risk having their manufacturing jobs automated away before they develop the service-sector and innovation capacity to absorb displaced workers. Policies that accelerate education quality and digital infrastructure expansion are critical.
4. **Regional and multilateral cooperation**: Middle-income countries individually lack the scale to shape global AI governance or attract frontier AI investment. ASEAN, AU, MERCOSUR, and similar regional bodies can coordinate AI strategy and data-sharing agreements.

**For Low-Income Countries:**
5. **Prioritise digital access as infrastructure**: Household internet access and mobile connectivity are prerequisites for any form of AI-augmented economic participation. These should be treated as essential public goods and funded through both domestic budgets and international development finance.
6. **Conditional technology transfer**: International climate and development finance frameworks (e.g., Just Energy Transition Partnerships) offer a model for embedding technology transfer, education, and institutional capacity-building alongside investment flows. Similar mechanisms for AI readiness support should be explored.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# References

-   Acemoglu, D. & Restrepo, P. (2020). Robots and Jobs: Evidence from US Labor Markets. *Journal of Political Economy*, 128(6), 2188–2244. [doi:10.1086/705716](https://doi.org/10.1086/705716)
-   Acemoglu, D. & Restrepo, P. (2019). Automation and New Tasks: How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor. *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, 33(2), 3–30. [doi:10.1257/jep.33.2.3](https://doi.org/10.1257/jep.33.2.3)
-   Autor, D. H. (2015). Why Are There Still So Many Jobs? The History and Future of Workplace Automation. *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, 29(3), 3–30. [doi:10.1257/jep.29.3.3](https://doi.org/10.1257/jep.29.3.3)
-   Brynjolfsson, E. & McAfee, A. (2014). *The Second Machine Age: Work, Progress, and Prosperity in a Time of Brilliant Technologies*. W. W. Norton.
-   Charrad, M., Ghazzali, N., Boiteau, V. & Niknafs, A. (2014). NbClust: An R Package for Determining the Relevant Number of Clusters in a Data Set. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 61(6), 1–36. [doi:10.18637/jss.v061.i06](https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v061.i06)
-   Frey, C. B. & Osborne, M. A. (2017). The Future of Employment: How Susceptible Are Jobs to Computerisation? *Technological Forecasting and Social Change*, 114, 254–280. [doi:10.1016/j.techfore.2016.08.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.techfore.2016.08.019)
-   Hayton, J. C., Allen, D. G. & Scarpello, V. (2004). Factor Retention Decisions in Exploratory Factor Analysis: A Tutorial on Parallel Analysis. *Organizational Research Methods*, 7(2), 191–205.
-   Horn, J. L. (1965). A Rationale and Test for the Number of Factors in Factor Analysis. *Psychometrika*, 30(2), 179–185.
-   Hubert, L. & Arabie, P. (1985). Comparing Partitions. *Journal of Classification*, 2(1), 193–218.
-   Kaiser, H. F. (1974). An Index of Factorial Simplicity. *Psychometrika*, 39(1), 31–36.
-   Kaufman, L. & Rousseeuw, P. J. (1990). *Finding Groups in Data: An Introduction to Cluster Analysis*. Wiley.
-   Le, S., Josse, J. & Husson, F. (2008). FactoMineR: An R Package for Multivariate Analysis. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 25(1), 1–18. [doi:10.18637/jss.v025.i01](https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v025.i01)
-   OECD (2023). *OECD Employment Outlook 2023: Artificial Intelligence and the Labour Market*. OECD Publishing, Paris. [doi:10.1787/08785bba-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/08785bba-en)
-   Oxford Insights (2025). *Government AI Readiness Index 2025*. Oxford Insights. [oxfordinsights.com/ai-readiness/ai-readiness-index/](https://oxfordinsights.com/ai-readiness/ai-readiness-index/)
-   Scrucca, L., Fop, M., Murphy, T. B. & Raftery, A. E. (2016). mclust 5: Clustering, Classification and Density Estimation Using Gaussian Finite Mixture Models. *The R Journal*, 8(1), 289–317. [doi:10.32614/RJ-2016-021](https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2016-021)
-   Sokal, R. R. & Rohlf, F. J. (1962). The Comparison of Dendrograms by Objective Methods. *Taxon*, 11(2), 33–40.
-   Tibshirani, R., Walther, G. & Hastie, T. (2001). Estimating the Number of Clusters in a Data Set via the Gap Statistic. *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Statistical Methodology)*, 63(2), 411–423. [doi:10.1111/1467-9868.00293](https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9868.00293)
-   World Bank (2024). *World Development Indicators*. World Bank DataBank. [databank.worldbank.org](https://databank.worldbank.org)
-   World Economic Forum (2023). *The Future of Jobs Report 2023*. World Economic Forum, Geneva.
-   Kaggle (2024). *Global AI Workforce Automation 2015–2025* (synthetic dataset). [kaggle.com](https://www.kaggle.com)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```

*This report was prepared as a midterm project for the Advanced Modelling course (Master in Computational Social Science, Universidad Carlos III de Madrid). All primary data are sourced from the World Bank World Development Indicators API, fetched reproducibly using the WDI package for R. Running this R Markdown document from source will regenerate every figure, table, and result presented above. Supplementary CSV files (Kaggle and Oxford Insights) must be placed in a `data/` subfolder.*
